title: Referral links & resource system (hardware devices + multisig services)
description: Admin-configurable referral/affiliate links for hardware wallet purchases (Trezor/Ledger/ColdCard/BitBox02/Jade), hardcoded official troubleshooting resources per device, and admin-configurable managed multisig service referrals (Casa/Nunchuk/Unchained/custom). Gated by new referral_links feature flag. Touchpoints: admin settings page, single-sig wizard device picker, multisig wizard, signing error/unavailable states, wallet detail device info. No tracking/analytics in v1. See scope doc discussed 2026-07-06.
close_reason: All 8 children built + closed (024542f, 8c974f7).

title: Backup restore doesn't filter sensitive settings keys
description: backup.ts:364-371 — restoreBackup iterates data.settings and upserts each key/value without filtering. buildBackup filters sensitive keys at export time, but restore has no equivalent filter. A crafted backup can inject registration_mode=open to re-enable open registration, or webhook_allow_private_targets=true to disable SSRF guard. Fix: apply SENSITIVE_SETTING regex filter on restore, or whitelist only exported setting keys.
close_reason: Fixed on track/restore @ 7a56d09, merged @ 068edc1, verified in final SHA 66f0c36; full evidence in comments.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Fixed on track/restore @ 7a56d09 (fix(backup): allowlist restorable settings, withhold security posture). Restore now applies a default-deny allowlist over data.settings; 5 posture keys withheld (registration_mode, webhook_allow_private_targets, instance_mode, auth_mode, electrum_tls_insecure). Skipped keys surfaced via settingsSkipped in the restore API response, the restore notification, and the admin backup UI. Test coverage: backup.test.ts +104 lines (new cases for allowlist enforcement). Merged into single-sig-full-wallet at 068edc1 (merge(security): integrate restore settings allowlist, cairn-0dg4, cairn-lka5). Full suite green post-merge (2320 passed / 1 skipped). Final pushed SHA: 66f0c36.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Reprioritized P2->P1 per 2026-07-12 beads audit (docs/BEADS-AUDIT.md sec 2/7): backup restore can inject registration_mode=open / disable SSRF guard, an auth-posture bypass with a small fix, underweighted at P2.

title: Add process-level crash guard (uncaughtException/unhandledRejection)
description: Systemic gap from review: no process.on(uncaughtException|unhandledRejection) anywhere, so a stray throw (SSE heartbeat setInterval, Electrum data-listener) killed the whole process for all users with no logged reason.
close_reason: DONE. Registered in two seams: server.mjs top-of-file (console.error fallback for the boot window before $lib alias resolves; guarded by listenerCount) and src/hooks.server.ts top-level (real logger via childLogger('process'); the one seam SvelteKit loads in every launch mode; replaces the fallback via removeAllListeners; globalThis flag prevents double-register under vite HMR). Policy: uncaughtException -> log + process.exit(1) (wallet app, never serve in undefined state, let supervisor restart); unhandledRejection -> log only (avoid crash loop from a benign stray rejection). Verified: node --check + npm run check clean; behavioral sanity check confirmed exit-code/log contract. Follow-up (optional, low prio): extract installProcessGuard(log,exit) for a unit test.

title: Wallet-creation wizard Step 4 (Name) also shows the full device selector, confusing first-time users
description:
Reported by Alex testing on phone, 2026-07-06. Step 4 of the single-sig wallet
creation wizard (src/routes/(app)/wallets/new/+page.svelte, step === 3, ~line
1044-1145) is titled "Step 4 - Name" but renders BOTH the wallet-name input AND
the full signing-device picker (DevicePicker, "Which device holds this key?")
on the same screen when the key was pasted rather than read from a connected
device (the `{:else}` branch at line 1115, when readMethod is 'paste' or the
user clicked "Change" on the device-confirm summary).

Alex's reaction hitting this on mobile: it reads as "did I need to upload my
key again? did I do something wrong?" -- combining a plain naming field with
what looks like a full re-import device selector on one screen is confusing
enough to risk wizard abandonment for a first-time user.

Fix: Step 4 should show ONLY "What should we call it?" and the name field.
Move the signing-device selection (which is already explicitly optional --
"Not sure? Leave it, you can pick when you send") to its own separate step,
shown after the name step and before wallet creation, or otherwise out of the
name screen entirely. The device-confirmed-from-read-key summary case (line
1093-1114, readMethod already known and not changed) can likely stay lightweight
since it is just a one-line confirmation with a "Change" link, not a full
picker -- the bug is specifically the full DevicePicker appearing alongside
the name field, not the confirmation summary.

Note: this file was just modified by cairn-alw8 (key-origin capture) and is
being actively touched right now by a concurrent session building
cairn-fdlf.1 (BIP-45 prefetch checkbox) in the same wizard -- coordinate to
avoid duplicate/conflicting edits to the same region.
close_reason: Duplicate of cairn-6hom (worktree mirror filed by the Wave B-1 agent, since this id didn't exist in its isolated worktree's beads DB at branch time). Fixed and merged in b7b2a4d: wizard Name step (Step 4) is now name-field-only; signing-device selection moved to its own Step 5. See cairn-6hom for full fix evidence.

title: No test coverage for wallet-config backup tracking (backups.ts, /api/wallets/[id]/config)
description: The cairn-dcp/iyj/lun6 backup-safety work (commits 7c9ab8b, f7848e4, a816047) shipped with zero automated test coverage: src/lib/server/backups.ts (markBackedUp/isBackedUp/listUnbackedWallets — the anti-join query and ON CONFLICT idempotency logic), the new GET /api/wallets/[id]/config endpoint (auth/ownership scoping, response shape), and the restore-file-parsing logic in wallets/new/+page.svelte (multisig-vs-single-sig detection heuristics) all lack tests. This is financial-safety-critical code per its own commit messages ('losing a wallet's config can mean permanently lost funds'), and the rest of the codebase has strong test discipline (664 tests, 30 files; the j6fv Tier-0 fix in the same session added a dedicated scriptMatch.test.ts). Add unit tests for backups.ts (idempotent markBackedUp, listUnbackedWallets anti-join correctness across both wallet kinds) and a request test for /api/wallets/[id]/config (ownership scoping — one user cannot fetch another's config; response contains no private key material).
close_reason: Added src/lib/server/backups.test.ts: 8 tests covering markBackedUp idempotency+timestamp refresh, isBackedUp, listUnbackedWallets (single-sig+multisig, per-user), and the 90-day reminder window/dismissal logic.

title: Wallets nav item leads to 404 — /wallets route not implemented
description:
CRITICAL: Clicking the "Wallets" sidebar nav item (visible to every logged-in user) navigates to /wallets, which returns a raw HTTP 404 rendered by SvelteKit's default unstyled error page ("404 / Not Found") — no Cairn branding, no nav, no way back except browser back button.

Repro: log in, click "Wallets" in the sidebar, or GET /wallets directly.

Root cause: no src/routes/(app)/wallets/** route exists yet (confirmed via glob — only +layout, explorer, settings, admin, and the app-level +page exist under src/routes/(app)/). Backend groundwork is partially in place (src/lib/server/wallets.ts, src/lib/server/bitcoin/walletScan.ts exist) but no frontend page/actions were wired up.

Impact: blocks the entire "Wallets" feature area for every user — import wizard, wallet detail, receive-address/QR generation are all untestable and unusable right now. The nav item should either be removed/hidden until the feature ships, or (better) the route should be built.

Also file separately: the default 404 error page itself is unbranded and should be replaced with a Cairn-styled error page app-wide.
close_reason: Fixed: wallet navigator implemented in 5595b82 — /wallets, import wizard, detail, and API all live and verified

title: hooks.server.ts has zero test coverage
description: hooks.server.ts — no hooks.server.test.ts exists. handleError 5xx leak contract, redactPath privacy redaction, /vaults->/wallets redirect (query string preservation for wizard resume), handle auth resolution, isAsset short-circuit — all untested. handleError is exported and trivially testable. A one-line regression leaks stack traces to clients. Fix: create hooks.server.test.ts with handleError leak test, redactPath boundary tests, redirect query preservation test.

title: Signing mass core: estimator, selection bias, split suggestion
description: src/lib/server/bitcoin/signingMass.ts: parent-tx vsize per input (reuse the construction path's cached fetchRawTx; lazy+cached for the UTXO-list endpoint), tier thresholds documented from pool stats, per-device signing-time estimates (documented rough constants; emulator harness can calibrate real numbers later), pool-batch heuristic (parent output count). Integrate: constructPsbt/vaultPsbt result gains signingMass per the epic contract; coin selection prefers low-mass UTXOs when candidates allow (never at the cost of higher fees — document the tiebreak); splitSuggested=true when total mass exceeds the timeout-risk threshold. utxo-mass endpoint. Tests incl. a synthetic 3000-output parent.
close_reason: Committed: signingMass.ts (28 tests, synthetic 3000/250/2-output parent archetypes), v2 contract on both construction paths (totalSeconds quorum-multiplied, warnLevel amber>600s red>1800s-or-Trezor-timeout-risk), pure estimateSigningSeconds for client surfaces, utxo-mass + signing-time-preview endpoints, fee-neutral selection bias, LRU parent cache. Tier thresholds 8k/40k vB reasoned from per-pool output counts. 568 tests green.

title: Wallet detail 'scanning history' banner never reflects real progress, stays stuck indefinitely
description:
Page: /wallets/[id] (wallet detail page), right after completing the import wizard.

What I did: imported a watch-only zpub via the 5-step wizard (Type -> Key -> Preview -> Name -> Import wallet), landed on the resulting wallet detail page at /wallets/1?imported=1, then reloaded the page (F5-equivalent) several seconds later once transaction/address data had clearly finished loading (50 transactions, 81 addresses already rendered in the tables).

What happened: the banner at the top of the page still read "Wallet imported - scanning history..." with a check-mark icon, even though the scan was obviously already complete (all 50 txs and 81 addresses were already displayed below it). The banner does not update or disappear on its own - it only disappears if the user manually clicks its dismiss (X) button. Root cause (confirmed by reading src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte and +page.server.ts): data.imported is driven purely by a one-time ?imported=1 query-string flag set by the redirect after import (url.searchParams.get("imported") === "1"), not by any real scan-status field. The banner text is a static string, not tied to actual scan state, and the query param persists across reloads of the same URL, so the misleading message can persist indefinitely.

What should have happened: the banner should reflect real state - e.g. disappear automatically once the scan actually completes (if there is a real async scan job), or at minimum not claim "scanning..." once the page has already rendered the fully-scanned transaction/address data in the same response. A user reloading the page minutes later and still seeing "scanning..." could reasonably think the import is stuck or broken.

Priority: P2 - cosmetic/trust issue, not data-breaking (the underlying wallet data is correct), but actively misleading for a product whose pitch is trustworthiness.
close_reason: Fixed in 5856144: banner now reports the completed scan result (tx and used-address counts) instead of claiming to still be scanning — the page only renders after the scan resolves — and the one-shot ?imported=1 param is stripped via replaceState so reloads don't resurrect it

title: Missing regression test: auth/webauthn/rateLimit security-event logging (cairn-wbmu)
description: cairn-wbmu (P1, closed in d56e77f) added logging to src/lib/server/auth.ts, webauthn.ts, and rateLimit.ts so failed logins, WebAuthn ceremony failures, and brute-force trips reach /admin/logs. No test asserts these log calls actually fire. Add a src/lib/server/security-logging.test.ts (or extend existing auth.test.ts/webauthn.test.ts) that: (1) triggers a failed password login and asserts a childLogger('security')-style warn/error log is emitted with userId/email context but no password; (2) triggers a failed WebAuthn verification and asserts a log call; (3) trips rateLimit's threshold and asserts a log call. This is a security-observability regression class — if a future refactor silently drops these calls again, nothing would fail.
close_reason: Regression tests committed in 56a1371 (security-logging.test.ts, auth.test.ts setSessionCookie block, logger.test.ts + exported REDACT_OPTIONS, agreement/server.test.ts, api/wallets/server.test.ts, (app)/layout.server.test.ts).

title: Resumed transaction drafts lose input/coin transparency
description:
Confirmed by reading src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/+page.server.ts and +page.svelte: when a user resumes a saved draft via ?tx=N (e.g. reloaded the page, or came back later to finish signing), the Review step review object falls back to a stub with inputs: [] and change: null (see the $derived.by review computation in +page.svelte around line 72-85), because the server only sends back a PsbtSummary (input/output counts, signedInputs, complete flag) via summarizePsbt(), not the actual per-input txid/vout/value or change details.

That data is not missing from the system -- it is sitting right there in the stored PSBT (transaction.psbt in the DB). psbt.ts summarizePsbt() already walks tx.getInput(i)/tx.getOutput(i) for the signed-count and outputs list; it just does not currently surface input txid/vout/amount (from witnessUtxo, or via the corresponding nonWitnessUtxo output for legacy inputs) or identify which output is change.

Impact: a user who builds a transaction, closes the tab, and comes back later to attach a signature or re-review before confirming sees "Input details load after building this session — resume from Create to see the exact coins" instead of the actual coins being spent -- on a page whose whole premise is "check every detail, this cannot be reversed." That is a real transparency regression specifically on the resume path, not the fresh-build path.

Fix: extend summarizePsbt (or add a sibling function) to also return per-input txid/vout/value and which output (if any) is change, then have +page.server.ts pass that through on resume so the Review step renders identically whether freshly built or resumed.
close_reason: Fixed: summarizePsbt returns per-input txid/vout/value (display-order txid verified by round-trip test) and identified change output — enabled by new change bip32Derivation tagging in constructPsbt (45f5425); Review step renders resumed drafts with full coin detail, null values degrade gracefully (7c944da).

title: Single-sig wallets read as spend-incapable: Watch-only label + no remembered signing device
description:
Reported by Alex, verified directly against the code.

What already works (correcting the initial assumption): src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/+page.svelte already has a prominent Send button (btn-primary, header) and the full hardware-wallet signing flow (Trezor, Ledger, ColdCard, QR, generic file) is already wired into single-sig send, not vault-exclusive. The Sign step already shows "Key 1 of 1" (line ~1047) -- single-sig is already a one-step signing stepper, that part does not need building.

The real gaps:

1. Misleading labeling. src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte line ~293 shows "Watch-only · {xpub}" as a subtitle directly beneath the wallet name, right next to the Send button. Cairn is watch-only in the technical sense (no private key touches the server) but that label reads as "cannot spend" to a normal user standing right next to a button that lets them spend. Reword away from "Watch-only" as the primary label -- something like "Wallet" or "Hardware wallet" with the no-private-keys detail moved to secondary/tooltip copy, not the headline under the wallet name.

2. No memory of which device signs this wallet. The wallets table (src/lib/server/db.ts, CREATE TABLE wallets) has columns id/user_id/name/type/xpub/script_type/receive_cursor/created_at -- no device_type column. Compare to the vault-keys table schema (same file, ~line 179): device_type TEXT, comment "routes the signing stepper" -- vaults already have exactly this mechanism, it was simply never carried over to single-sig wallets. Consequences:
   - The wallet import flow (wallets/new/+page.svelte) only ever asks for xpub + name -- never which device holds the key.
   - Every single-sig send requires re-selecting a signing method from scratch (Trezor vs Ledger vs ColdCard vs QR vs file) with zero memory of the previous choice, even though a given watch-only wallet is virtually always paired with the same one device long-term.
   - Device descriptions in the Sign step (send/+page.svelte deviceMethods array, ~line 556-591) are generic vendor blurbs ("Sign on-device over USB via Trezor Connect...") rather than personalized ("Sign with your Trezor") the way a remembered-device wallet could read.

Fix, mirroring the pattern that already exists for vaults: add a nullable device_type column to wallets (same enum as vault-keys: trezor|ledger|coldcard|qr|file|null). Prompt for it once -- either at import time as an optional step, or the first time a user reaches the Sign step without one set ("Which device holds this key?") -- and persist the answer so subsequent sends pre-select it and the Sign step can say "Sign with your Trezor" by name instead of presenting the full device picker every time. Keep the picker reachable for wallets that genuinely rotate devices or were never told one.

P1 per Alex: single-sig is the most common wallet type in the app and currently looks more limited than it actually is.
close_reason: Verified fixed across 819419c, f2503bb, 829c28c, cf6654e: wallets table now has a device_type column (with a migration for existing rows), threaded through WalletRow/WalletSummary/createWallet plus a setWalletDevice helper. send/+page.svelte pre-selects the remembered device (walletDevice = data.wallet.deviceType) and renders personalized 'Sign with your <device>' copy instead of the generic vendor blurb. All user-facing 'watch-only' wording is gone -- remaining hits are internal code comments explicitly noting the design intent ('never watch-only, since it can always sign'), which is correct practice, not a leftover. Null device_type correctly falls back to the universal file/PSBT method rather than blocking anything.

title: Client keeps unbounded /api/sync polling storm + perpetual SSE churn -- page never network-idles
description:
Evidence: dev instance 2026-07-12, continuous rapid POST/GET requests to /api/sync observed with no backoff when the backend chain source is unreachable, alongside persistent /api/notifications/stream and /api/events SSE connections that keep churning (reconnecting) rather than settling. Beyond QA-tooling noise, no-backoff client polling on a sustained error condition is a real cost (battery/CPU/network on real deployments) and means the page never reaches network-idle even when nothing is happening.

Searched for overlap first: cairn-3bt1 (address-watcher baseline race / notification flood, CLOSED) and cairn-u7bw (Electrum client no keepalive, CLOSED) are both server-side Electrum/addressWatcher issues, not the browser client's /api/sync or SSE polling cadence -- different root cause and different layer. cairn-sp74 (OPEN, P2, 'Electrum reconnect backoff bypassed by ambient requests') is the closest related bead but is specifically about server.ts ensureConnected() bypassing its own backoff timer on the Electrum socket -- not the browser's client-side polling loop against /api/sync or the notifications/events SSE endpoints. cairn-pk85 (SSE reconnect hardening for NotificationPanel, CLOSED) added visibility-change/stale-timer reconnect logic to the notifications SSE client but this report is about polling storm/no backoff under sustained failure, which is a distinct symptom. No exact match found -- filing new.

Likely owned by the security-hardening / client-server boundary workstream (per earlier note, workstream-b). Client-side work: SyncBanner's /api/sync poller (introduced in cairn-2zxt.1) and the notifications/events SSE consumers need capped exponential backoff when requests are failing, so an unreachable backend doesn't produce a continuous request storm.

title: addressWatcher scripthash-keyed state has no generation/ownership tag — cross-wallet clobber on scripthash reuse
description: Pre-existing design edge noted by review of cairn-uzgu (finding #7) and cairn-mo36's builder. state.byScripthash / baselinedScripthashes / inFlight are keyed by scripthash string with no generation counter or wallet-ownership tag. Two consequences when the SAME scripthash/xpub is reused across a delete+recreate (or an xpub shared by two wallets): (1) refreshWatches' subscribe loop skips re-adding because a stale (wrong-walletId) byScripthash entry is still present, so the new wallet's deposits attribute to the stale entry until walletStillExists self-prunes it — dropping one notification; (2) a stale in-flight handler's finally { state.inFlight.delete(scripthash) } can clear a NEW handler's in-flight marker for the same scripthash. Both are narrow, self-healing, and pre-existing (not introduced by uzgu/mo36). Proper fix: tag watcher state entries with a generation/ownership id (kind+walletId) and match on it in inFlight cleanup + the refreshWatches subscribe skip, rather than keying purely by scripthash string.

title: C:/Program Files/Git/recover page with rate limiting
description: 'Lost your passkey?' link on login. Flow: email -> recovery phrase OR one code -> verified -> register new passkey -> in. Rate limit 5 attempts/hour (per account AND per IP; constant-time comparisons; no user-existence oracle in error copy).
close_reason: Shipped (last commit): /recover page + login 'Lost your passkey?' link; email -> phrase-or-code -> verify -> register new passkey -> signed in; generic non-enumerating errors, graceful rate-limit, account-only reassurance copy.

title: Multisig BIP-48 derivation path audit (2026-07-06)
description:
Audit of BIP-48 derivation-path handling in Cairn multisig (creation + Caravan import), compared against C:\dev\bastion. Requested by Alex. Scope: path/script-type consistency, path separation between wallet types, upload/import flow validation.

FINAL MODEL (per Alex, 2026-07-06, superseding earlier drafts in this bead's history)
==========================================================================
1. Collaborative custody multisig — a vault where cosigner keys are SHARED
   with other people, set up together ON-PLATFORM (multiple Cairn users, or a
   Cairn user + external cosigners, jointly building one wallet) — ALWAYS
   requires BIP-45 (m/45') xpubs for every freshly-derived key. Non-negotiable
   for this creation path. When the user connects a hardware wallet during
   this flow, Cairn auto-derives m/45' — no path picker, no ambiguity. This is
   deliberate defense-in-depth: BIP-45's purpose' field (45') itself signals
   "this key is meant for collaborative custody" to any cosigner or external
   tool inspecting the wallet, independent of whatever script type the vault
   ends up using. Alex is explicit this is "good practice even if you believe
   its deprecated" — the choice is intentional, not a mistake to correct.
2. Self-made multisig — one user, all their own keys, a personal/local vault
   never shared with other people — uses standard BIP-48 paths
   (m/48'/coin'/account'/script_type'), chosen per the vault's actual script
   type. This is Cairn's existing, already-correct behavior (see the file-level
   audit below) and needs no change.
3. Single-sig xpubs (BIP-44/49/84/86) are NEVER valid as a multisig cosigner
   key, full stop — this applies universally, to every acceptance path
   (fresh creation AND import), no exceptions.
4. Imported multisig configs (Caravan JSON, ColdCard export, descriptor import,
   etc.) are NOT required to conform to the BIP-45 rule above. An imported
   wallet already exists on-chain with whatever multisig-purpose path (45' or
   48') it was actually built with — Cairn must faithfully represent it, not
   reject or coerce it. "All imported wallets can be used for collaborative
   regardless of path" (Alex, verbatim). The BIP-45-required rule in point 1
   applies ONLY to vaults Cairn itself is deriving fresh keys for during
   on-platform collaborative creation — it is a creation-flow product rule,
   not a general import-validation rule. Point 3 (reject single-sig purposes)
   still applies to imports; point 1 (require exactly 45', not 48') does not.
5. Manual xpub paste can't be re-derived or verified against a live device, so
   it needs its own guided flow: ask collaborative-vs-personal intent first,
   tell the user which path to export (m/45' for collaborative) with concrete
   device instructions, then validate the pasted path's prefix against the
   declared intent and reject/warn on mismatch, sending the user back to their
   device to re-export from the right path. See cairn-fdlf.5.
6. Bastion reference (confirmed by direct source read, not assumption): Bastion
   asks upfront ("I plan to share this key... later") and branches which
   path(s) to pull based on the answer — msDefaultPath()/wireDeviceConnect()
   in public/js/views/wallets.js. This ask-intent-then-derive shape is exactly
   what Cairn needs to port; see cairn-fdlf.4 for the multisig-wizard version
   and cairn-fdlf.1 for the single-sig-wizard prefetch version.

Priority: Alex has called out getting all of this collaborative-custody
derivation-path work built as a priority — beads under this epic and cairn-fdlf
that implement points 1/4/5 above have been bumped to P1 and are meant to be
ready for direct implementation (flagged "ready for Fable to build").

Summary of gaps found (all confirmed by direct code read, not just subagent report):
- No check that a cosigner's declared path purpose is a valid multisig purpose
  (45' or 48') at all — a single-sig-derived path (44'/49'/84') is accepted
  into a multisig with no complaint (cairn-1kc3.3).
- No check that a cosigner's declared BIP-48 path suffix (4th component: 1' vs
  2') matches the wallet's actual scriptType, at creation or import time — and
  this check only makes sense for 48'-purpose keys; BIP-45 has no script-type
  subfield (cairn-1kc3.1).
- createMultisig's own sanity-check derivation (multisigTestAddress) omits
  scriptType entirely, so it always validates as if p2wsh regardless of the
  wallet's real script type (cairn-1kc3.2).
- No cross-wallet-type xpub reuse detection — the same xpub can be stored as
  both a single-sig `wallets` row and a `multisig_keys` cosigner row (or across
  multiple multisigs); no DB constraint or app check catches it (cairn-1kc3.4).
- Caravan import forces network=mainnet but never cross-checks the coin_type
  embedded in each 48'-purpose key's bip32Path against that — N/A for
  45'-purpose keys, which have no coin_type field (cairn-1kc3.5).
- No product-level enforcement of "on-platform collaborative creation must use
  BIP-45" or the ask-intent UX to get there in the first place — new scope,
  see cairn-fdlf.4 and cairn-fdlf.5.

Bastion (src/server/wallet-manager.ts) has the same validation gaps — this is
not a regression Cairn introduced, it's an unaddressed class of validation
missing from both. Fix in Cairn only per Alex's ask; do not touch Bastion.

Related but separate: cairn-alw8 (P0) is the foundational bug that single-sig
wallets never persist master_fingerprint at all, breaking hardware-wallet PSBT
signing entirely. That bug does NOT affect this epic's scope (multisig keys
already store fingerprint correctly) but is a prerequisite for any of this
path-hygiene/UX work to matter in practice — there's no point deriving the
perfect BIP-45 key if the resulting PSBT still can't be signed. Fix cairn-alw8
first; this epic's P1 beads can and should proceed in parallel/after since they
don't touch the same code.

Child beads carry per-issue file:line detail and fix sketches.
close_reason: All 6 units (cairn-1kc3.1 through .6) built and merged: cosigner path-purpose validation, BIP-48 script-type-suffix check, scriptType passed through to the sanity-check derivation, cross-wallet xpub-reuse detection, Caravan coin_type cross-check, and collaborative-vault BIP-45 hard enforcement. See docs/MULTISIG-DERIVATION-AUDIT-2026-07-06.md for the original audit. Found and spun off one new bug along the way: cairn-opo6 (descriptor import hardcodes p2wsh).

title: Multisig accepts cosigner paths whose BIP-48 script-type suffix contradicts the wallet's scriptType
description:
BIP-48 reserves the 4th path component to say what script type a key is for: .../1' = P2SH / P2SH-P2WSH, .../2' = P2WSH. Cairn never checks that a cosigner's declared path matches the multisig's actual scriptType, at either creation or import.

SCOPE NOTE (per Alex's final collaborative-custody model, 2026-07-06, see
cairn-1kc3 epic): this check applies ONLY to 48'-purpose keys — i.e. self-made/
personal multisig vaults, where Cairn derives standard BIP-48 paths per the
vault's chosen script type. It does NOT apply to 45'-purpose keys (on-platform
collaborative vaults, which per Alex ALWAYS use BIP-45 regardless of script
type — BIP-45's structure, purpose'/cosigner_index/change/address_index, has no
script-type subfield at all, by spec). validateDerivationPath() (see
cairn-1kc3.3) must branch on the purpose (pathIndexes[0]) before applying this
suffix check: run it for 48'-purpose paths, skip it entirely for 45'-purpose
paths.

Evidence:
- src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts:151-164 (parsePath) parses syntax only — no check against scriptType.
- src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts:187-216 (resolveKey) stores pathIndexes but never compares them to config.scriptType.
- src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts:221-228 (multisigScriptType) only validates the scriptType string itself, never cross-references key paths.
- src/lib/server/wallets/multisig.ts:212-252 (createMultisig) has no suffix check before insert.
- src/lib/server/multisigExport.ts:262-295 (parseCaravanImport) copies `bip32Path` straight into the key record (line 293) with zero validation — not even parsePath syntax checking, let alone suffix-vs-addressType.

Concretely: a Caravan JSON declaring `"addressType": "P2WSH"` with a cosigner `"bip32Path": "m/48'/0'/0'/1'"` (the P2SH suffix) is accepted silently by both the import preview and createMultisig. The resulting wallet's on-chain script is still correctly P2WSH (script type is taken from config.scriptType / descriptor wrapper, not recomputed from the path), but the stored path is now a lie that will be shown to the user, embedded in exported descriptors/PSBTs, and handed to hardware wallets for path display — creating a mismatch against every other tool (Coldcard, Sparrow, the actual signing device) that DOES derive script expectations from the path, and undermining the "cross-check against another descriptor tool" verification step multisigTestAddress's own comment describes (multisig.ts:372-373).

Fix: add a validateDerivationPath(pathIndexes, scriptType) check — when
pathIndexes[0] === 48 + HARDENED and pathIndexes.length >= 4, indexes[3]
(unhardened) must be 2 for p2wsh and 1 for p2sh/p2sh-p2wsh; when pathIndexes[0]
=== 45 + HARDENED, skip this check entirely (no script-type field to validate).
Called from resolveKey (bitcoin/multisig.ts) so it fires for both creation and
import (since createMultisig ultimately calls multisigTestAddress ->
resolveMultisig -> resolveKey). Also add the same check earlier in
parseCaravanImport so the import preview surfaces the error with per-key
attribution instead of a generic one at creation time.
close_reason: 48'-purpose paths now require the 4th component to match the wallet scriptType (2' = p2wsh, 1' = both p2sh forms) inside validateCosignerKeyPath; 45'-purpose paths are skipped entirely (no script-type subfield, per the scope note). Also requires length >= 4 for 48' paths. Fires at createMultisig and parseCaravanImport (per-key attribution) and the API descriptor-import branch. The audit's concrete case (Caravan P2WSH file with bip32Path m/48'/0'/0'/1') is now rejected with the key named — pinned in multisigExport.test.ts and bitcoin/multisig.test.ts. Existing round-trip fixtures in multisigExport.test.ts that carried exactly this lie (p2sh configs with 2'-suffix keys) updated to honest paths.

title: createMultisig's own address-derivation sanity check ignores the wallet's scriptType
close_reason: createMultisig now builds ONE config object ({threshold, scriptType, keys}) that feeds both validateMultisigKeyPaths and multisigTestAddress, so the sanity derivation exercises the wallet's real script type instead of defaulting to p2wsh (src/lib/server/wallets/multisig.ts). Regression test: wallets-multisig.test.ts 'accepts a p2sh wallet whose keys carry the 1-suffix' — proves scriptType flows into the acceptance config (with 1kc3.1's check, a dropped scriptType would wrongly reject 1'-suffix keys on a p2sh vault and wrongly accept 2'-suffix ones; both directions asserted).

title: No multisig-purpose check — single-sig-derived paths (44'/49'/84'/86') are accepted into multisig cosigner slots
description:
BIP-45 (m/45'/cosigner_index/change/address_index) and BIP-48 (m/48'/coin'/account'/script_type') are the two legitimate purpose values for a collaborative-custody (multisig) key in Cairn — both signal "this key is meant for a shared/multisig wallet," distinct from single-sig purposes (44'/49'/84'/86'). Per Alex's decision (2026-07-06), Cairn will support BOTH:
- BIP-45 as the "shared key" marker/derivation — the purpose field itself (45') is what tells cosigners this key is meant for collaborative custody. Matches Bastion's actual mechanism (opt-in checkbox -> second device read at m/45', see cairn-fdlf.1/.4).
- BIP-48 as the script-type-aware derivation for the real wallet's cosigner keys, adding coin_type/account/script_type structure that BIP-45 lacks.

Cairn never checks the purpose field (the 2nd-from-top component, i.e. indexes[0] in parsePath's output) is 48' OR 45' when accepting a multisig cosigner key — today it accepts anything, including bare single-sig purposes.

Evidence: same call sites as cairn-1kc3.1 — src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts resolveKey/resolveMultisig and src/lib/server/multisigExport.ts parseCaravanImport — neither inspects pathIndexes[0].

Concretely, a cosigner key declared with path "m/84'/0'/0'/0/0" (a single-sig receive-address path, not even an account-level path) is accepted today with no complaint, at both createMultisig and Caravan import. Combined with cairn-1kc3.4 (no xpub-reuse detection across wallet types), this means the SAME xpub a user already uses for a single-sig wallet can be pasted into a multisig with its original single-sig path intact and nothing flags it.

Why this matters beyond validation hygiene (per Alex, 2026-07-06): this is the
exact mechanism by which single-sig path info could leak into the collaborative
custody flow. Collaborative custody wallets MUST use a multisig-purpose path
(45' or 48') — never a single-sig path. Two concrete harms if this is left unchecked:
1. Firmware recovery: a multisig cosigner record with a bogus (single-sig) path
   means a future from-seed recovery attempt (device + Sparrow/Specter/Coldcard,
   no Cairn) will derive the WRONG key for that slot, since every other tool
   trusts the recorded path to know what to re-derive. This isn't cosmetic —
   it can make a wallet unrecoverable outside Cairn.
2. Unnecessary information exposure: once a single-sig path is embedded in a
   multisig key record, it flows into every export (Caravan JSON, Coldcard file,
   descriptor) and therefore to every cosigner who receives that export — leaking
   information about a wallet the cosigner has no business knowing about. See
   cairn-fdlf.3 for the sharing-side half of this (what gets shared by default);
   this bead is the input-validation half (stop it from getting in at all).

IMPORTANT structural difference between the two accepted purposes — the checks
in cairn-1kc3.1 (script-type suffix must match wallet scriptType) and
cairn-1kc3.5 (coin_type must match mainnet) are BIP-48-SPECIFIC and do not apply
to a 45'-purpose key: BIP-45 has no coin_type or script_type field at all (its
structure is purpose'/cosigner_index/change/address_index — script type for a
BIP-45-derived multisig wallet is a wallet-level choice, not encoded in the
path). validateDerivationPath() must branch on pathIndexes[0]:
  - 48 + HARDENED -> require length >= 4, validate indexes[3] against scriptType
    (1kc3.1) and indexes[1] against expected coin_type (1kc3.5)
  - 45 + HARDENED -> valid on its own; no further per-index checks apply
  - anything else (or a single-sig purpose) -> reject
  - empty path / bare "m" -> stays allowed, matching current behavior for xpubs
    with no recorded origin

Fix: implement validateDerivationPath() as above, called from resolveKey
(bitcoin/multisig.ts) so it fires for both creation and import, and from
parseCaravanImport so the import preview surfaces the error with per-key
attribution instead of a generic one at creation time.

SCOPE BOUNDARY: this bead only rejects single-sig purposes (44'/49'/84'/86')
from multisig cosigner slots — it accepts BOTH 45' and 48' equally, at both
creation and import, with no preference between them. It deliberately does NOT
enforce "on-platform collaborative creation must be exactly 45', never 48'" —
that stricter, creation-flow-only product rule (imports are exempt) lives in
cairn-fdlf.4, alongside the ask-intent UX that gets the user to the right path
in the first place.
close_reason: validateCosignerKeyPath() added in src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts: rejects any declared cosigner path whose purpose is not 45' or 48' (single-sig 44'/49'/84'/86' named per BIP in the error; unhardened 45/48 and unknown purposes also rejected; 'm'/empty and Caravan masked all-zero paths still allowed). Enforced at BOTH acceptance boundaries: createMultisig (wallets/multisig.ts — covers wizard + API create + import-create) and parseCaravanImport (multisigExport.ts, per-key attribution) + descriptor branch of POST /api/wallets/multisig/import. Placement deviation from the fix sketch: NOT in resolveKey — resolveMultisig runs on every derivation/export of STORED wallets, so validating there would brick pre-existing wallets that carry a bad path (funds unviewable, descriptor unexportable). Acceptance-gate placement documented in code comments. Regression tests: bitcoin/multisig.test.ts (validateCosignerKeyPath suite incl. the audit's m/84'/0'/0'/0/0 case), multisigExport.test.ts (Key-N attribution), wallets-multisig.test.ts (creation + import-source). Full suite 1479 passed, npm run check 0 errors.

title: No detection when the same xpub is reused across a single-sig wallet and a multisig cosigner slot
description:
Schema check (src/lib/server/db.ts):
- wallets: UNIQUE (user_id, xpub) — line ~55
- multisig_keys: UNIQUE (multisig_id, xpub) — line ~296

Both uniqueness constraints are scoped to a single wallet/multisig. Nothing (DB constraint or app code) stops the same xpub from being stored in a `wallets` row AND a `multisig_keys` row for the same user, or from appearing as a cosigner in two unrelated multisigs of different script types.

cosignerDetection.ts (src/lib/server/cosignerDetection.ts) matches on FINGERPRINT across a user's accepted contacts, but that's a UX feature ("this cosigner key might belong to your contact X") — it explicitly does not check the importing user's own existing wallets, and matches by fingerprint (which is shared across every path from one seed) rather than flagging same-xpub reuse specifically.

Risk: a user (or an app bug, or a malicious Caravan file) can register one xpub under two different derivation contexts — e.g. as a single-sig p2wpkh wallet AND as a p2wsh multisig cosigner. Nothing in Cairn warns the user this key is already committed elsewhere, which is exactly the scenario BIP-48 path separation exists to prevent tooling from silently accepting.

Fix: before createWallet (wallets.ts) or createMultisig (wallets/multisig.ts) insert, check whether the same user_id already has this xpub in the *other* table (or another multisig) and surface a non-blocking warning (not necessarily a hard reject — reuse might be intentional in edge cases, but it should never be silent). A simple `SELECT 1 FROM wallets WHERE user_id=? AND xpub=? UNION SELECT 1 FROM multisig_keys k JOIN multisigs m ON m.id=k.multisig_id WHERE m.user_id=? AND k.xpub=?` covers both directions.
close_reason: detectXpubReuse() added in src/lib/server/cosignerDetection.ts: for a given user, finds each offered xpub already stored as a single-sig wallets row OR a cosigner key in another of their multisigs, comparing on CANONICAL xpubs (SLIP-132 Ypub/Zpub aliases match; canonicalXpub() newly exported from bitcoin/multisig.ts). Chose warn-not-block per the bead ('reuse might be intentional... should never be silent'): createMultisig computes reuse BEFORE insert (so the new vault never matches itself) and records a level=warn 'key_reuse' activity event naming the wallets involved (no xpubs in message/detail); 'key_reuse' added to USER_FEED_TYPES in activity.ts so it genuinely surfaces on /activity + the bell. Both API routes (create + import) also return xpubReuse in their responses (preview and 201) for future wizard UI. Tests: cosignerDetection.test.ts (detectXpubReuse suite: both tables, SLIP-132 both directions, cross-user isolation) and wallets-multisig.test.ts (feed warning appears, self-match excluded, disjoint silent).

title: Caravan import doesn't cross-check a cosigner path's coin_type against the (mainnet-only) network field
description:
src/lib/server/multisigExport.ts:219-225 hard-rejects any Caravan import whose top-level `network` field isn't "mainnet" (Cairn only tracks mainnet). But it never looks at the coin_type embedded in each key's bip32Path (the 2nd component, e.g. the "1" in m/48'/1'/0'/2').

SCOPE NOTE (per Alex's final collaborative-custody model, 2026-07-06, see
cairn-1kc3 epic): coin_type only exists in the BIP-48 path shape
(m/48'/coin'/account'/script_type') — this check applies ONLY to 48'-purpose
keys. BIP-45 (m/45'/cosigner_index/change/address_index) has no coin_type field
at all, so a 45'-purpose key is automatically exempt from this check, the same
way it's exempt from the script-type-suffix check in cairn-1kc3.1.

Concretely: a Caravan JSON with `"network": "mainnet"` but every 48'-purpose key's `"bip32Path": "m/48'/1'/0'/2'"` (coin_type=1, the testnet convention) imports successfully today. That's a strong signal the file was copy-pasted from a testnet export and hand-edited, or generated by a buggy tool — worth surfacing as an error rather than silently accepting.

Lower priority than cairn-1kc3.1/.3 since Cairn's mainnet-only restriction limits blast radius, but cheap to add alongside the other path checks in this epic.

Fix: as part of validateDerivationPath() (see cairn-1kc3.1/.3), when
pathIndexes[0] === 48 + HARDENED, also accept an expected coin_type parameter
and check pathIndexes[1] === 0 + HARDENED for mainnet (Cairn's only supported
network today). Skip entirely when pathIndexes[0] === 45 + HARDENED.
close_reason: validateCosignerKeyPath enforces coin_type for 48'-purpose paths: indexes[1] (hardened or not) must be 0 (mainnet, Cairn's only network); 45'-purpose paths exempt (no coin_type field, per scope note). Fires in parseCaravanImport with per-key attribution (the bead's target — a mainnet Caravan file with m/48'/1'/0'/2' now fails naming the key) and equally at createMultisig and descriptor import. Tests: multisigExport.test.ts 'rejects a testnet coin type inside a mainnet file', bitcoin/multisig.test.ts coin-type cases.

title: Persist vault mode (collaborative vs personal) on multisigs and hard-enforce BIP-45-only server-side
description:
Gap identified 2026-07-06 while reviewing whether the collaborative-custody
BIP-45 requirement (cairn-1kc3 epic, cairn-fdlf.4/.5) is actually a hard
invariant or just a client-side nudge. As currently scoped, cairn-fdlf.4/.5
only ask intent and validate in the WIZARD — nothing at the data layer stops a
vault from being recorded as "collaborative" while actually holding 48'-purpose
(or worse, single-sig) keys, if the wizard is bypassed, buggy, or called
directly via the API. Alex called the BIP-45 requirement "non-negotiable" —
this bead makes that true server-side, not just true in the happy-path UI.

Two things needed:

1. SCHEMA — a new column on `multisigs` recording the user's declared intent
   at creation time: "do this vault's cosigner keys come from multiple
   different people" (collaborative) vs "these are all my own keys"
   (personal). Cairn already has a `source` column (`'created'` | `'imported'`,
   src/lib/server/db.ts:851-862, added for backup-prompt gating) — this is a
   DIFFERENT axis and must not be conflated with it: a fresh on-platform vault
   can be personal or collaborative; an imported vault's path is exempt from
   this entirely regardless (see below). Also do not confuse this with the
   EXISTING `multisig_shares` table (multisigShares.ts, `role: viewer|cosigner`)
   — that's Cairn's in-app access-sharing feature for an already-created
   wallet, unrelated to derivation-path intent at creation time. Suggested:
   `collaborative INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0` on `multisigs`, set once at
   creation (src/lib/server/wallets/multisig.ts:254 is where `source` is
   already resolved and inserted — add `collaborative` alongside it, same
   INSERT). No UI to change it after creation — flipping it after the fact
   wouldn't retroactively fix already-derived key paths anyway.

2. SERVER-SIDE ENFORCEMENT — createMultisig() (src/lib/server/wallets/multisig.ts:212-260)
   gains a `collaborative?: boolean` param alongside the existing `source` param
   (params object, :214-225). Before/alongside the existing multisigTestAddress()
   cryptographic sanity check (:249-252):
   - If `collaborative === true` AND `source !== 'imported'`: every key in
     params.keys MUST resolve to purpose === 45' + HARDENED via
     validateDerivationPath() (cairn-1kc3.3) — throw MultisigError
     ('invalid_key' or a new 'requires_bip45' code) otherwise. This is the hard
     backstop cairn-fdlf.4/.5's UX validation currently lacks.
   - If `collaborative` is false/undefined: unchanged, existing BIP-48
     script-type/coin_type checks apply (cairn-1kc3.1/.5).
   - If `source === 'imported'`: skip this check entirely regardless of the
     collaborative flag's value — imports are exempt from the BIP-45
     requirement no matter what ("all imported wallets can be used for
     collaborative regardless of path," Alex, 2026-07-06).
   Any OTHER code path that adds a key to an existing collaborative multisig
   later (if one exists or gets added) must apply the same check — this isn't
   a one-time gate only at initial creation.

Depends on cairn-1kc3.3 (validateDerivationPath() must exist first). Related to
cairn-fdlf.4 (the wizard is the source of the `collaborative` intent this bead
persists and enforces) and cairn-fdlf.5 (paste flow — same enforcement applies
whichever path added the key).

Scoped as P1 alongside the other collaborative-custody-priority beads, but
Alex has signed off that this can be refined/adjusted later — the point right
now is to have it tracked before this goes to build, not to have every detail
final.
close_reason: Persistence + enforcement half built (wizard UX half remains cairn-fdlf.4/.5, untouched per Wave-A scope). SCHEMA: guarded ALTER adds nullable 'collaborative INTEGER' to multisigs (db.ts, next to the source migration; NULL = never declared — deliberately nullable rather than the sketch's NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, so pre-existing rows and mode-less flows stay 'undeclared' instead of being silently marked personal). Mapped through MultisigRow.collaborative (boolean|null) in wallets/multisig.ts. ENFORCEMENT in createMultisig: new optional collaborative param (accepted by POST /api/wallets/multisig and the import route); when source!=='imported' and collaborative===true every key must resolve to purpose 45' via cosignerPathPurpose() (unknown-origin 'm' keys rejected too — they can't prove BIP-45, and the 45' marker IS the point); collaborative===false rejects 45' keys (they mark a key as shared) while unknown-origin stays allowed; null/omitted = today's behavior; imports exempt from mode enforcement regardless but the flag still persists. No other code path inserts multisig_keys post-creation (verified: only createMultisig + backup restore), so the creation gate is currently complete. Tests: wallets-multisig.test.ts 'createMultisig vault mode (cairn-1kc3.6)' — all six branches.

title: Admin invites: 'Create' button intermittently does nothing on click (~2/3 failure rate)
description:
Page: /admin/invites

What I did:
- As admin, filled the "Create invites" form (or left it at defaults: How many=1, Uses each=1, Expires in days=30) and clicked the "Create" button repeatedly across several fresh page loads.

What happened:
- Out of 6 real mouse clicks on the Create button (across multiple fresh page loads, including one where I waited a full 3 seconds after load before clicking to rule out a hydration-timing issue), 4 did nothing at all: no new invite appeared in the table, and `read_network_requests` confirmed literally zero network activity — not even a failed/rejected request. No console errors were logged either.
- By contrast, calling `document.querySelector('form.create-form').requestSubmit()` directly via JS worked instantly and correctly created an invite, every time I tried it. This rules out a server-side or form-validity problem — all fields were valid (checked via `checkValidity()` on every input, all true) and the POST target (`?/create`) is correctly wired.
- The failure isn't tied to page-load timing (still failed after a 3s wait) or exact pixel coordinates (both a "miss" and a "hit" happened at click coordinates within 1px of each other, on different attempts). It looks like a genuinely intermittent client-side issue — most likely the button's click handler (whatever wraps the native submit for progressive enhancement / use:enhance) is sometimes not attached or not firing, rather than the browser's native form submission being blocked.
- Interestingly, an immediate second click right after a failed one usually succeeded (retrying without reloading fixed it), which is why this could easily be missed in casual manual testing — a real admin who clicks once, sees nothing, and clicks again would likely not even notice this is a bug.

What should have happened:
- Every click on "Create" should reliably submit the form and create an invite. A ~67% first-click failure rate (4 of 6 in this session) on a core admin action — inviting new users — is a serious reliability problem, especially since it currently fails silently with no error, toast, or loading indicator to tell the admin anything went wrong.

Priority: P1 — this blocks/confuses the primary onboarding path (admin invites a second user) for most Cairn instances, and fails completely silently, which will read as "the button is broken" to a real admin. This is exactly the kind of button flakiness worth root-causing before shipping a collaborative-custody-focused release.
close_reason: Fixed: root cause is the spinner/icon swap under the pointer on rapid clicks — pointerdown on the spinner, response swaps it back before pointerup, browser fires no click (zero network, no error; requestSubmit unaffected). Global .btn>svg/.spinner pointer-events:none routes clicks to the button; explicit type=submit added. Could not repro with synthetic CDP clicks (3/3 succeeded), consistent with a real-pointer-coalescing race; the fix removes the race class app-wide. Browser-verified button still submits.

title: walletApi.ts completely untested — untrusted PSBT entry point
description: walletApi.ts — readSpendRequest parses untrusted spend-request JSON for both single-sig and multisig PSBT builders. feeRate: Number(body.feeRate) and amount: toAmount() get no local finite/positive/bounds validation (NaN/negative/Infinity pass through). Coin-control and batch feature-gate enforcement exists only here. No route test exercises it either. Fix: add walletApi.test.ts with bounds validation tests and feature-gate enforcement tests.
close_reason: Merged 0b5d534: walletApi.test.ts covers readSpendRequest shaping, psbtBuildErrorResponse mapping, classifyUtxoMasses, backupFileResponse. No production bug found. Note: file doesn't itself parse/match PSBTs (that's downstream) — tested the real surface.

title: Worker threads: measure first, likely gate behind Phase 1/derivation fixes landing
description:
server.mjs is single-threaded, zero worker_threads anywhere; single DatabaseSync singleton (WAL already on, busy_timeout+foreign_keys already set). CPU-bound work sharing the main loop: address derivation, QR generation, notification queue, watchers, retention sweeps, all synchronous SQLite calls.

Fable's synthesis explicitly disagrees with "do workers first": after the derivation-memoization bead lands, main-thread CPU cost of derivation collapses, and QR generation is per-request and small -- offloading them may be optimizing a fixed problem. GATE this bead behind a post-wave-1-3 measurement on the real Umbrel/ARM box (perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay histogram + timing wrappers around doWalletScan phases).

If measurement shows it's still needed: Stage 1 (low risk) = pure CPU work with no shared DB handle (QR gen, raw EC/HDKey derivation if still hot). Stage 2 (bigger, gated on the sync-engine Phase 1 bead consolidating the sync engine first) = move scan orchestration into a worker pool, each worker with its OWN DatabaseSync connection (a DatabaseSync handle cannot be passed across threads). Gotchas: busy_timeout AND foreign_keys=ON are both per-connection -- must reissue both on every worker connection or silently lose FK enforcement; busy_timeout doesn't save a read-then-write upgrade from SQLITE_BUSY -- workers doing read-then-write need BEGIN IMMEDIATE not bare BEGIN; on SD-card storage, long-lived reader snapshots across connections can starve WAL checkpointing and balloon the -wal file -- keep worker read transactions short.

Explicitly REJECTED: moving SSR rendering into worker_threads (adapter-node's handler is tightly coupled to raw http.IncomingMessage/ServerResponse, would need a Piscina-scale per-request pool, disproportionate for a Pi target). Also REJECTED for this epic: SSR->CSR rewrite (lag is scan storms + sync DB on request path, not rendering -- the portfolio and sync-engine beads already remove the blocking work from SSR) and node:sqlite->better-sqlite3 swap (both synchronous, swap doesn't unblock the event loop which is the actual complaint in cairn-xlrm, adds native-module ARM build pain for zero gain -- the real answer is worker Stage 2 above, later).

title: Jade multisig registration: 16-char name truncation with no hash suffix causes silent on-device collisions
description: src/lib/hw/jade.ts: sanitizeJadeMultisigName (~line 318) truncates the sanitized wallet name to a fixed 16 chars with no uniqueness/hash suffix, and registerMultisigWithJade (~line 598) never checks for an existing on-device registration before writing (jadets exposes getMultiSigName()/getRegisteredMultisigs() for exactly this content-based dedup lookup, per node_modules/jadets/dist — neither is called). Two multisig wallets whose names share a 16-char prefix after sanitization (e.g. 'Family Vault Multisig Primary' / 'Family Vault Multisig Backup' -> both 'FamilyVaultMulti') silently collide: per Jade's fixed-slot device-side registration semantics, re-registering an existing name REPLACES the prior entry with no error, so the first wallet's on-device registration is silently overwritten and later signing/address-verification for it produces wrong results with no error surfaced. This exact risk was independently flagged in this project's own scout research recorded on beads issue cairn-vxi (deterministic name = sanitized prefix + 6-hex sha256 of the canonical descriptor, requires firmware >=1.0.27 for getMultiSigName dedupe) and is required by docs/HARDWARE-PLAN.md (line ~310, lists jade.getMultiSigName as a required operation) — the shipped code does not implement either mitigation. Not yet wired into any UI call site, so this is latent in unreleased code, but it is unit-tested to preserve the vulnerable truncation behavior (jade.test.ts asserts exact 16-char truncation) — fix before wiring this up to a real signing flow.
close_reason: registerMultisigWithJade now derives the device-side registration name from the wallet's canonical descriptor (jadeMultisigRegistrationName: sanitized 9-char prefix + 6-hex sha256 of the order-independent descriptor identity, within Jade's 16-char limit). Two different wallets sharing a 16-char name prefix now register under distinct names, so Jade's fixed-slot registration can't silently overwrite the first wallet's entry. Same wallet -> same name (idempotent re-registration); cosigner order doesn't affect it. Unit tests cover the collision and ordering cases. sanitizeJadeMultisigName retained (used for the prefix) so its existing test still holds.

comments.author: auditor
comments.text: Escalating: commit a130de2 wired this into a REAL signing flow — MultisigJadeUsbSigner.svelte's connectAndSign() calls registerMultisigWithJade({name: multisigName, ...}) with the raw user-chosen wallet name on every sign attempt (called repeatedly/idempotently by design), still with no dedup/collision check and no call to jadets' own getMultiSigName()/getRegisteredMultisigs() (confirmed still unused anywhere in the codebase). This is no longer latent/unwired driver code — it's now live-exercised every time a user signs a multisig transaction with a Jade. Two wallets whose sanitized names share a 16-char prefix will silently clobber each other's on-device registration the moment both are used for signing on the same Jade, undermining the on-device address-verification guarantee the signing UI heavily emphasizes ('check the address on the Jade's screen'). Recommend treating as higher priority now that it's reachable via normal use, not just theoretical.

title: Signup: empty invite code gives no error message, just silent focus
description: Clicking 'Create account' with the invite code field left blank does not submit (good, client-side validation), but it gives zero feedback beyond an orange focus outline on the field -- no 'this field is required' text. A beginner may not notice the subtle outline change and think the button is broken.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: signup form now has novalidate so validate() surfaces a styled 'invite code required' (and every other field) message instead of only a native focus outline (src/routes/(auth)/signup/+page.svelte).

title: Multisig wizard has zero reload resilience — loses all key-ceremony progress on Umbrel proxy reload
description: wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte:807-817 — comment says 'This wizard always starts there (no mid-flow resume)'. No sessionStorage snapshot, no beforeunload guard. Same Umbrel auth-proxy reload trigger that forced the single-sig wizard rebuild (v0.1.2, wizardProgress.ts) applies here, but cost-per-loss is HIGHER: each cosigner key (2-of-3 up to 15) often needs a physical hardware-device ceremony, so a mid-flow reload after adding 4 of 5 keys means redoing 4 device ceremonies — reads to a non-expert as 'the wallet is broken' at peak-trust moment, a safety regression (some abandon multisig). Fable ranked this the #1 UX fix (impact-to-effort: the seam is already built+tested+shipped). TWO-PHASE (Fable): PHASE 1 this release — port the wizardProgress.ts sessionStorage seam (covers the exact same-tab proxy-reload trigger, near-zero cost). PHASE 2 next — persist wizard state SERVER-SIDE as a draft committed AFTER EACH KEY (per-key commit, not save-on-exit), resumable via URL like the send flow's ?tx= draft; it's all PUBLIC key material (xpub/fingerprint/path) so no secret-handling burden, spans hours/days ('add the Coldcard when I'm home'), survives tab-close/crash/device-switch, and becomes the foundation collaborative-custody needs anyway.
close_reason: Phase 1 landed in 4d447fe. wizardProgress.ts sessionStorage seam (cairn.multisig-wizard.v1, 60-min TTL) mirrors single-sig; rejects whole snapshot on any bad key, clamps step/quorum mismatch to keys step; wired restore/snapshot/clear-on-create/resume-banner. Phase 2 (server-side per-key drafts) remains — filing follow-up. Tests: wizardProgress 22/22, single-sig 15/15 no regression.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: IMPLEMENTED 2026-07-07 (PHASE 1 only) — UNCOMMITTED. New _components/wizardProgress.ts mirroring the single-sig seam (key cairn.multisig-wizard.v1, 60-min TTL): persists step/quorum/keys[]/vaultMode/import-config/name; rejects the WHOLE snapshot on any bad key (a silently-dropped cosigner is undetectable), clamps step/quorum mismatch back to 'keys'. Wired into +page.svelte: restore-before-seedStepHistory, snapshot , clear-on-create, resume banner. FABLE decisions: exclude the in-flight add-key form (device handles die on reload anyway; loss capped at one uncommitted key); cooperates with cairn-aiyw popstate (no race — pushState/replaceState never fire popstate); + Fable caught two gotchas the agent implemented (loadPreview on restore-to-review, known-keys refetch on restored vaultMode). Phase 2 (server-side per-key drafts) remains open per original scope. Evidence: multisig wizardProgress 22/22, single-sig 15/15 (no regression), full multisig/new dir 40/40; svelte-check clean.

title: Contacts: minor comment-accuracy and observability gaps
description:
Two minor, non-security findings from reviewing commit d7b915b's contacts.ts:
1. requestContact's file header/JSDoc claim ALL branches return 'the same success shape,' but requesting your own email returns a distinguishable 400 {error, code:'self'} (contacts.ts:80-82) rather than the uniform 200 — not an enumeration vector against other users (triggering it requires already knowing you're targeting your own account), but the comment overclaims uniformity. Low-impact; matches Bastion's reference implementation's equivalent deliberate 'you can't add yourself' UX choice, so likely intentional — just update the comment to carve out this one exception rather than claiming full uniformity.
2. contacts.ts has no childLogger usage and never calls recordActivity()/notify() for request/accept/decline/remove events, unlike the sibling multisigRoster.ts from the same commit family which does log and notify for its analogous events. A contact-request recipient gets no activity-feed entry or notification and must manually check Settings > Contacts to discover a pending request. Not a security issue, just inconsistent with the project's stated observability conventions.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: contacts.ts now uses childLogger for request/accept/decline/remove and records a recipient activity-feed event on new/accepted requests (contact_request/contact_accepted whitelisted in USER_FEED_TYPES). The requestContact JSDoc already carves out the self-email 400 exception, so item 1 needed no change.

title: Send flow swallows requireFeature's 403 message (reads body.error, server sends body.message)
description:
Found during end-to-end browser testing of the admin feature-flags system (2026-07-06),
as the companion bug to cairn-jyh7 (coin_control has no client-side UI gate).

src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/+page.svelte build(), around line 339:
    const body = await res.json();
    if (!res.ok) {
        buildError = body.error ?? 'Could not build the transaction.';
        return;
    }

requireFeature() in src/lib/server/api.ts calls SvelteKit's error(403, def.userMessage),
which serializes to JSON as { message: "..." }, not { error: "..." } -- confirmed live:
  curl .../api/wallets/6/psbt -> {"message":"Coin control has been disabled by your administrator."}

So any requireFeature 403 that reaches this fetch (concretely: submitting the send
form with manual coins selected while coin_control is disabled, see cairn-jyh7) reads
body.error, gets undefined, and falls back to the generic
"Could not build the transaction." -- the user never learns the real reason
their transaction was rejected.

Other failure paths in the same handler (e.g. insufficient-funds, which the wallet's
own code returns as { error: "..." }) display correctly, so this is specifically a
mismatch with SvelteKit's error() helper's response shape.

Fix: in the build() catch branch, prefer body.message (SvelteKit error()) with a
fallback to body.error (app-thrown errors), e.g.
  buildError = body.message ?? body.error ?? 'Could not build the transaction.'
Worth grepping other client fetch handlers under src/routes/(app)/wallets for the
same body.error-only pattern in case it recurs elsewhere.
close_reason: Fixed in commit b1bc8e9: both single-sig and multisig send pages now read body.error ?? body.message in build/sign/broadcast handlers, so a requireFeature 403 (SvelteKit error() → {message}) surfaces the real 'disabled by your administrator' reason instead of the generic fallback.

title: Missing regression test: dashboard portfolio scans run concurrently, unverified (cairn-lo8)
description: cairn-lo8 (P1, closed in 6f1ce4f) fixed the dashboard portfolio load scanning wallets sequentially by switching to Promise.all in /api/portfolio. No src/lib/server/portfolio.test.ts exists (the sibling portfolioWarm.test.ts covers a different function, the warm-cache pass, not this). Add portfolio.test.ts: mock scanWallet/scanMultisig with artificially delayed promises for 3+ wallets, call getPortfolioDetail(), and assert wall-clock time is close to one delay rather than the sum — proving concurrency, not just correctness.
