- non-admin: banner shows 'Retry' only, no settings link
Screenshot tool was unavailable this session (known hazard); used the read_page/get_page_text fallback per prior QA notes. Both throwaway users + their sessions/recovery/agreement rows deleted after QA -- confirmed zero rows remaining.

title: sendBoundaryDraft multisig boundary test flakes on 5s timeout under full-suite load
description: src/lib/server/sendBoundaryDraft.test.ts:299 ('multisig: one sat short of amount+fee rejects; the exact amount succeeds changeless') times out at the default 5000ms during a full npm test run, but the whole file passes in isolation in ~5.1s (12/12). Root cause is CPU contention under the parallel full suite pushing this multisig seed+buildDraft test past the per-test ceiling, not a logic bug. Fix: bump testTimeout for this file/case (e.g. 15000ms) or lighten the multisig seeding. Found during cairn-6efi.1 Wave 1 work; unrelated to explorer changes.

title: SOCKS5+TLS: no timeout on the TLS handshake over the proxy tunnel
description: Follow-up to cairn-vn48 (landed ed95397). The connect-timer added there covers only the two DIRECT dial branches. On the SOCKS5+TLS branch (socks5Host set AND useTls), SocksClient's timeout option bounds the SOCKS CONNECT, but the subsequent TLS handshake over the established tunnel is unbounded — a backend that completes the tunnel then stalls the TLS handshake could still hang ensureConnected() forever (proxy/Tor users only). Fix: extend the connect-timer to also cover the TLS-over-tunnel handshake (arm around the wrapTls(tunnel) step in the SOCKS+TLS path), or add a dedicated handshake deadline there.
close_reason: Fixed in 275f751 (worktree branch worktree-agent-a76bdb15881777d63). armConnectTimeout()/disarmConnectTimeout() now wrap the wrapTls(tunnel) TLS-over-SOCKS5-tunnel step at src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts:313-315 (previously only the two direct dial branches at ~324 and ~328 were armed). Reuses the existing onReady/fail machinery, no double-fire/leak risk (full connect path read and verified). Confirmed the plain-TCP-over-SOCKS5 sub-path (tunnel already connected, setImmediate(onReady)) cannot hang -- unchanged, request-level timeout already bounds the handshake there. New test: src/lib/server/electrum/client.test.ts 'rejects instead of hanging forever when the TLS handshake over an established SOCKS5 tunnel never completes (cairn-ocs9)', using a real SOCKS5 proxy fixture + mocked tls.connect. Full suite: 2032 passed, 1 skipped, 0 failed (npx vitest run). svelte-check: 0 errors, 0 warnings.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Fix landed on single-sig-full-wallet as 3fae3de (cherry-picked from worktree commit 275f751 referenced in the close comment above).

title: TODO comment references an internal, non-public issue-tracker ID
description: src/lib/server/bitcoin/signingMass.ts line ~160: "TODO(cairn-194): calibrate against real hardware..." references a local beads-tracker issue ID. .beads/ is explicitly gitignored and not pushed to the public repo (see the earlier repo-hygiene decision to untrack it) -- so this ID is meaningless and unresolvable to any external contributor reading the code, a dangling reference to a system that doesn't exist from their side. Either drop the specific ID and just describe the follow-up work in plain prose, or (once the project has public GitHub issues) reference a real public issue number the way OSS projects conventionally do (TODO(#123): ...).
close_reason: Fixed: tracker reference removed, substance kept as prose, stale harness path corrected.

title: Multisig-creation wizard collapses 3 distinct operations into one generic error message with no errorId
description: src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte:294-298's callAction() helper backs three distinct operations (adding a cosigner key, importing a Caravan config, previewing an address) with one shared "Something went wrong -- try again." fallback and no errorId for support correlation -- this is exactly the setup flow a self-hosting non-expert is most likely to get stuck in on their first wallet, with no path forward if retrying doesn't help. Fix: give each operation its own fallback message, and/or add an errorId matching the pattern used by hooks.server.ts's handleError. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: callAction() now takes a per-operation fallback message; add-key/import/preview/create each pass their own, replacing the single generic 'Something went wrong' (wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte).

title: Suppress (app) chain/sync banners on onboarding flow pages (recovery-setup, disclosure)
description:
recovery-setup inherits the (app) layout, so ChainHealthBanner/SyncBanner render above what should be a pure onboarding wizard -- these are chain-connectivity banners with no relevance during recovery setup, and they can visually stack with the page's own form-error surfaced by the auto generatePhrase $effect. This is a DIFFERENT root cause than cairn-7zjo (which deduped two banners firing for the same underlying cause); here the banners shouldn't be rendering on this route at all.

Suggested fix: add a hideChainBanners flag on flow/onboarding routes, or a !isFlowPage guard at (app)/+layout.svelte:119-126, so recovery-setup, disclosure, and similar onboarding-wizard pages never render the chain/sync banners.

Found-by: cairn-7zjo design review (deep-reasoner), 2026-07-12.

title: notification_channel_config has no getPublicChannelConfig() redaction guard yet — forward risk for Units 9/10
description: commit c9483a6 (Notify Unit 1) shipped the notification_channel_config schema (src/lib/server/db.ts:553) with a comment promising 'Never returned to the client verbatim (see getPublicChannelConfig)', but no such function exists anywhere in the repo (confirmed by grep — only the comment and docs/NOTIFICATION-PLAN.md's prose reference it). No route reads the table yet, so there is no live leak today. But there is nothing structurally stopping a future Unit 9/10 author from doing SELECT config FROM notification_channel_config and returning it raw to a client-facing load function or API route, which would leak SMTP passwords/webhook HMAC secrets/ntfy tokens verbatim to the browser — exactly the class of bug this codebase already shipped once for coreRpcPass before getPublicInstanceSettings() (src/lib/server/settings.ts:65-70) fixed it with a hasCoreRpcPass boolean-flag pattern. Fix: add a getPublicChannelConfig() now (even as groundwork ahead of Units 9/10) that mirrors getPublicInstanceSettings()'s redaction pattern, so the secret-handling contract is enforced in code rather than just a comment.
close_reason: Added src/lib/server/notifyConfig.ts with redactChannelConfig() and getPublicInstanceNotificationSettings(), the getPublicChannelConfig()-style single source of truth. Wired all 4 former hand-rolled redactors (settings/notifications loader, channel API route, admin API route, admin page loader) to it.

comments.author: auditor
comments.text: Update: commit 6c3d1ec (notification settings + admin UI, Units 9&10) shipped and its message claims 'secrets redacted like coreRpcPass' — but getPublicChannelConfig() still does not exist anywhere. Instead there are now 4 independent hand-rolled redaction implementations (redact() in settings/notifications/+page.server.ts, redactForClient() in api/notifications/channels/[channel]/+server.ts, publicNotificationSettings() in api/admin/notifications/+server.ts, and a 4th copy duplicated inline in admin/notifications/+page.server.ts). Traced every current secret field (ntfy accessToken, webhook secret, smtp_pass, telegram_bot_token) through all 4 paths and confirmed each is currently redacted correctly — not an active leak today. But the fragmentation is worse than originally flagged: 4 places to keep in sync instead of 1, with nothing in the type system forcing a future secret field to be added to all of them. Recommend consolidating into one shared getPublicChannelConfig()-style helper now, mirroring settings.ts's getPublicInstanceSettings()/hasCoreRpcPass, before a 5th call site or a new channel's secret field gets added.

title: Missing/inadequate regression test: admin overview wallet count omits multisig wallets (cairn-xqfb)
description: cairn-xqfb (P2, closed in c110211) fixed the admin overview 'Wallets' count only summing the single-sig wallets table, omitting multisig wallets. A test exists in admin.test.ts (describe('instanceStats', ...) around line 172-179) but its fixture has zero rows in both the wallets and multisigs tables, so it cannot actually distinguish 'sums both tables' from 'counts only one' — it would pass against the pre-fix buggy code too. Strengthen the existing test: insert one row into wallets and one into multisigs for different users, then assert instanceStats().wallets === 2, so the test would genuinely fail against a regression back to counting only one table.
close_reason: Regression tests committed in 834945e (admin.test.ts, notifiedTxidsCleanup.test.ts, settings.test.ts, api/admin/{settings,users,notifications}/server.test.ts). All green in full suite.

title: Admin settings dual write surface: form action ?/save and PUT /api/admin/settings hand-mirror the same validation
description: src/routes/(app)/admin/settings/+page.server.ts (?/save action) and src/routes/api/admin/settings/+server.ts (PUT handler, comments say it 'mirrors the validation') both write the same setting keys and both call reconfigureChain(). The duplicated validation logic must be kept in sync by hand across the two files, which is a drift risk (e.g. a new setting or validation rule added to one surface but not the other). Extract a shared validate+apply module (suggest src/lib/server/settingsWrite.ts) that both the form action and the REST endpoint call, so the two surfaces can't diverge. NOTE: this is not dead code -- do NOT propose removing the REST endpoint, it serves API-token automation.

title: Hardware wallet signing
description:
Device-specific signing integrations, each plugging into the guided signing stepper framework (cairn-yph.6) and the PSBT infrastructure epic (cairn-yph). Per Alex: bring over all the hardware wallets from Bastion (C:\dev\bastion). Important scope correction from the Bastion survey: Bastion actually has Ledger, Trezor, and Jade signing implemented, plus BitBox02 (not requested here, FYI only) -- but ColdCard signing is NOT implemented in Bastion, only a descriptor-registration file export. ColdCard's real SD-card sign workflow needs to be built from scratch for Cairn, using the air-gapped/file-based patterns as a starting point rather than a direct port.

Suggested build order within P2 (all same priority per Alex's instruction, but this is the natural dependency-free-to-hardest ordering): generic file-based signing first (no vendor SDK, exercises the whole stepper+PSBT round-trip end to end and immediately usable with Sparrow/Electrum/any PSBT wallet) -> Trezor Connect (web SDK, no native transport complexity) -> Ledger (WebHID) -> air-gapped QR (SeedSigner/Passport/Jade) -> ColdCard (needs the most from-scratch design work).

Security pattern to carry over into every device integration below, from Bastion's psbt.ts applyPartialSignatures(): never blindly trust a signature a device returns. Validate the returned signature's pubkey against the PSBT's own bip32Derivation fields before accepting it as valid for a given input. This is the difference between a hardware wallet integration and a hardware wallet integration with a spoofing hole in it.
close_reason: Epic complete: all five signing methods live in the Sign step — generic file, Trezor Connect (5f2d59a), Ledger WebHID (87c6ba6), air-gapped QR/BBQr (677c767), ColdCard SD card (e1d3d52), wired in 0f59954+a092c0f. Every device routes through the server-side substitution guard; every driver validates returned signatures against the PSBT's own bip32Derivation pubkeys per the epic's security note. npm run check 0 errors, 275 tests passing.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
Adversarial browser verification (for S5's audit ask re: keys never touching Cairn / signatures validated against PSBT derivation / broadcast gate actually blocking):

1. No private key/seed ever touches Cairn: confirmed across all 5 signer components (generic file, Trezor Connect popup, Ledger WebHID, ColdCard SD file, QR/BBQr camera+paste) — every one of them deals exclusively in PSBTs. None has any input field, prompt, or code path that accepts a seed phrase or raw private key. Key custody stays entirely on the external device/Connect popup in every flow.

2. Signature validation, not blind trust: live-tested by uploading a PSBT with a tampered output amount through both the ColdCard file-upload path and the QR manual-paste path (real oncall wallet, real /file endpoint bytes, modified via @scure/btc-signer). Both were rejected server-side: "The signed transaction pays different outputs than the one you reviewed. It was not accepted." Also tested uploading a still-unsigned PSBT — rejected with "This PSBT was attached but still is not fully signed," confirming the client only advances to Confirm after the server (assertSameTransaction + summary.complete) actually validates it. This matches the epic's stated security pattern.

3. Confirmation gate before broadcast: PATCH-attach and broadcast are two separate server calls (transactions.ts), each independently re-running assertSameTransaction + finalizePsbt — so even a client that skipped the UI gate couldn't get an insufficiently-validated PSBT broadcast. Didn't attempt a real mainnet broadcast (no funded test wallet available + out of scope for browser testing without explicit sign-off), but the guard logic itself was exercised directly against real code in a prior session's oracle proof (constructPsbt -> sign -> assertSameTransaction -> finalizePsbt, full pass).

Also found and filed cairn-sta (P2, non-security): ColdCard/QR signer cards don't proactively warn when a wallet lacks stored key-origin data, unlike Ledger/Trezor which do — walks the user through physical device steps for a predictably-doomed signing attempt. Everything else checks out clean.

title: Generic file-based signing (universal fallback)
description: Download the constructed PSBT as a file, let the user sign it in any external PSBT-compatible tool (Sparrow, Electrum, a hardware wallet's own desktop companion app), then upload the signed result back into Cairn to finalize and broadcast. This is the universal path that works for literally any signing setup, needs no vendor SDK integration, and should be built first since it exercises the full construction -> stepper -> signed-upload -> broadcast round trip end to end without any device-specific complexity. This is effectively the same mechanism as PSBT download/upload (cairn-yph.2) with signing-flow framing/copy layered on top -- do not build a second implementation of the download/upload mechanics.
close_reason: Satisfied by the guided stepper's Sign step (commit 40cf0d8) — the 'Generic wallet / file' method fully implements download-unsigned / sign-externally (Sparrow, Electrum, ColdCard, any HW companion) / upload-or-paste-signed. Per the bead, this is the same mechanism as yph.2 with signing-flow framing, not a separate build. Verified live end to end: .psbt download (200 octet-stream), signed upload normalized from both binary-base64 and hex, mismatch guard, unsigned->incomplete. This is the universal fallback every other device path falls back to

title: Trezor Connect integration
description: Web-based signing via Trezor Connect -- user approves the transaction on-device over USB, no file export/import needed. Bastion reference: C:\dev\bastion\public\js\trezor.js (452 lines), using @trezor/connect-web (remote-loaded JS, opt-in). Bastion has a server-side /trezor/prepare -> /trezor/apply seam -- understand why before copying it verbatim; may or may not be needed depending on how Cairn structures its PSBT signing request/response. Must include on-device address/amount verification in the guided flow (the stepper's Confirm step should tell the user to check the destination and amount on the Trezor screen itself, not just trust the browser UI).
close_reason: Trezor Connect signing shipped: driver src/lib/hw/trezor.ts (5f2d59a) with client-side PSBT->Connect translation (no server prepare/apply seam needed — Cairn parses PSBTs client-side), device-account-vs-bip32Derivation pubkey verification before attributing positional signatures, DER+sighash merge-back; TrezorSigner.svelte with on-device verify instructions; wired into Sign step (a092c0f). 27 unit tests; check+tests green.

title: Ledger integration (WebHID)
description: Web-based signing via Ledger's Bitcoin app over WebHID. Bastion reference: C:\dev\bastion\public\js\ledger.js (334 lines), using @ledgerhq/hw-transport-webhid + ledger-bitcoin. Note Bastion persists a per-wallet, per-device 'policy HMAC' (schema.ts wallet.ledger_registrations table) for multisig registration -- not needed for Cairn's initial single-sig-only scope, skip that complexity for v2 and revisit if/when Cairn ever supports multisig wallets. Must include on-device verification step in the guided flow, same as Trezor.
close_reason: Ledger WebHID signing shipped: src/lib/hw/ledger.ts driver + LedgerSigner.svelte (87c6ba6), wired into Sign step gated on isWebHidAvailable with disabled-reason fallback (0f59954). Policy-HMAC multisig registration skipped per bead scope (single-sig only). 25 unit tests; browser-verified by tester.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Ledger WebHID lib + LedgerSigner.svelte committed (87c6ba6); Sign-step integration pending

title: ColdCard SD card workflow
description: Export unsigned PSBT to a file the user copies to an SD card (or via the newer virtual-drive-over-USB firmware, out of scope for v1 of this feature -- SD card only), signs on the ColdCard itself, then copies the signed PSBT file back and uploads it into Cairn. IMPORTANT: unlike the other four device beads in this epic, Bastion does NOT have this built -- it only exports a descriptor-registration file (coldcardMultisigFile() in wallet-manager.ts), no actual signing round-trip. This needs to be designed from scratch, not ported. It's mechanically closest to the generic file-based signing bead (cairn-ogw.1) -- same download/upload primitive -- the ColdCard-specific work is entirely in the guided-flow instruction copy (SD card insert/remove steps, PSBT filename conventions ColdCard expects) rather than new signing logic.
close_reason: ColdCard SD-card workflow shipped: ColdCardSigner.svelte file round-trip with guided SD insert/sign/re-import instructions (e1d3d52), wired into Sign step (0f59954). Designed from scratch as bead notes required (no Bastion port); reuses the download/upload primitive + substitution guard.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: ColdCardSigner.svelte SD-card flow committed (e1d3d52); Sign-step integration pending

title: Air-gapped QR signing (SeedSigner, Passport, Jade)
description:
Show the unsigned PSBT as an animated/paginated QR code (device camera scans it, fully air-gapped, no USB/file transfer), then scan the signed-PSBT QR the device displays back using the browser's camera (BarcodeDetector API or a JS QR-reading fallback for browsers without it). Bastion reference for the QR encode/scan mechanics: C:\dev\bastion\public\js\hw-signer.js (BBQr encoding via bbqrEncode + BBQrReassembler for the scan-back side) -- SeedSigner and Passport both use this same QR path in Bastion.

IMPORTANT scope note on Jade specifically: Bastion's actual Jade integration (C:\dev\bastion\public\js\jade.js, 534 lines) uses Web Serial (wired USB-serial transport, jadets library), NOT QR -- even though Jade the device supports true air-gapped QR signing as a hardware feature. This is a real decision point, not an oversight to just copy: Web Serial is proven/working in Bastion and is a faster port, but Web Serial has narrower browser support (no Safari, no mobile) than camera-based QR scanning, and true air-gapped QR is arguably the more fitting choice alongside SeedSigner/Passport in this bead's own grouping. Decide and note which transport Jade gets in Cairn before starting -- don't silently default to whichever is easier without that being a deliberate call.
close_reason: Air-gapped QR signing shipped: BBQr encode/scan lib + QrSigner.svelte animated multi-frame display, BarcodeDetector camera scan-back with per-frame progress and paste fallback (677c767), state_unsafe_mutation fix (d9d75c9), wired into Sign step (0f59954). Jade decision: camera QR (BBQr) not Web Serial — broader browser support, matches SeedSigner/Passport grouping. Tester verified full encode->sign->scan-back round trip with real BIP84 test vector.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: BBQr encode/scan lib + QrSigner.svelte committed (677c767); Sign-step integration pending. Jade transport decision: camera QR (BBQr), not Web Serial — matches SeedSigner/Passport grouping and broader browser support

title: No SOCKS5/Tor proxy support for the Electrum/Esplora chain backend
description:
Found during a privacy-tools parity audit against Bastion (2026-07-06) — neither codebase has this, but it's a real, material gap for Cairn specifically given its stated privacy-conscious design philosophy (plain language, no exposed internals, user-controlled backups).

src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts connects to the configured Electrum server via raw tls.TLSSocket/net.Socket (no proxy support at all). src/lib/server/settings.ts's electrum config (electrumHost/electrumPort/electrumTls/electrumTlsInsecure) has no proxy fields. Same story for the Esplora HTTP path.

Impact: every address/balance/UTXO query Cairn makes leaks the operator's real IP address to whichever Electrum/Esplora server is configured — including third-party public servers if the operator points at one instead of running their own node. A self-hosted Bitcoin wallet with no Tor option is a meaningful privacy gap; most privacy-oriented wallets (Sparrow, Electrum itself, Wasabi) offer at least a SOCKS5 proxy setting for exactly this reason.

Recommended fix: add a socks5Host/socks5Port (or torProxy) field to instance settings, and wrap the Electrum TCP connection (and Esplora HTTP client) in a SOCKS5 CONNECT handshake when set — a well-trodden pattern (e.g. the 'socks' npm package, or a raw SOCKS5 client, ~100 lines) that doesn't require bundling Tor itself, just the ability to point at a local tor SOCKS proxy (default 127.0.0.1:9050) or any other SOCKS5 proxy.
close_reason: Added SOCKS5/Tor proxy support for the chain backend end-to-end. Settings: new socks5Host/socks5Port on InstanceSettings + settings.ts (socks5_host/socks5_port), surfaced in getChainConfig() in BOTH public and custom modes (proxy is independent of connection mode). Electrum: ElectrumClient dials via socks.SocksClient CONNECT when a proxy is set, with TLS negotiated end-to-end over the tunnel (proxy sees only ciphertext); hostname sent to proxy for remote DNS (no leak). Esplora: EsploraApi routes fetch through socks-proxy-agent (socks5h://, remote DNS) via a node http/https path since global fetch/undici can't take a SOCKS dispatcher. Admin UI: new 'Privacy: SOCKS5/Tor proxy' field group on /admin/settings (always visible), with validation in both the form action and the JSON API; Test buttons exercise the proxy too. Declared socks + socks-proxy-agent as explicit deps. Tests: 2 new Electrum E2E tests (dials through an in-test SOCKS5 proxy; errors when proxy unreachable). Full suite 1059 pass, typecheck clean. Note: in-browser admin-page verification not driven (admin-gated); relied on svelte-check + unit tests per this branch's build constraints.

title: Settings desktop breadcrumb back-button loop — pushes history instead of replacing
description: Desktop breadcrumb links (viewport >=900px) at src/routes/(app)/settings/tokens/+page.svelte:41, settings/devices/+page.svelte:36, settings/contacts/+page.svelte:121, settings/notifications/+page.svelte:518 render a plain `<a class="crumb-link" href="/settings">`. This pushes a new history entry, so pressing Back from the sub-page returns to the sub-page itself (alternating-back loop) - same bug class fixed for mobile in cairn-y7ac via BackCircle.svelte using goto(href, {replaceState:true}). Fix: apply the same replaceState navigation pattern to the desktop breadcrumb links (or extract a shared BreadcrumbLink component).
close_reason: Fixed via commit 346827c (worktree agent-a17e2b1327fd84733). All 4 desktop settings breadcrumb links (tokens/devices/contacts/notifications) now use goto(href, {replaceState:true}) on click, matching BackCircle.svelte's mobile fix pattern exactly. vitest 1880/1 skipped, svelte-check 0/0.

title: Undefined danger/danger-muted design tokens with wrong-semantic fallbacks
description:
Confirmed by grep: var(--danger, ...) and var(--danger-muted, ...) are used in 4 places across 3 files, but --danger/--danger-muted are never defined in src/app.css. CSS custom-property fallback silently kicks in, with inconsistent results per call site:
- src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/send/+page.svelte:1454 and src/routes/(app)/vaults/[id]/send/+page.svelte:1423 -- color: var(--danger, var(--text)) -- a "danger" indicator silently renders as plain neutral text, no red/alarm color at all.
- src/routes/(app)/vaults/stateless/+page.svelte:1486,1491 -- var(--danger-muted, var(--warning-muted)) and var(--danger, var(--warning)) -- renders as yellow warning instead of red danger, semantically wrong severity.

This is not new to vaults -- the wallets/[id]/send/+page.svelte usage predates the vault feature, it was just never caught until this audit went looking for it specifically. Fix: add real --danger / --danger-muted tokens to app.css (most likely just alias them to the existing --error / --error-muted tokens, since "danger" and "error" are the same semantic red status here -- decide whether to standardize naming on --error everywhere and drop the --danger references, or keep both names as aliases for readability at call sites).
close_reason: Fixed in d5f9c73: added --danger and --danger-muted to src/app.css as aliases of --error/--error-muted, so the var(--danger)/var(--danger-muted) call sites resolve to the correct red instead of falling back to neutral text or yellow warning.

title: Descriptor import hardcodes scriptType p2wsh — sh(wsh()) and sh() descriptors create a wallet with the wrong script type
description: src/routes/api/wallets/multisig/import/+server.ts parseSource() and src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/+page.server.ts import action both hardcode scriptType: 'p2wsh' for the descriptor branch, with a comment claiming 'parseDescriptor only accepts wsh(sortedmulti(...))'. That comment is stale: parseDescriptor (src/lib/server/bitcoin/multisig.ts) accepts sh(wsh(sortedmulti())) -> p2sh-p2wsh and sh(sortedmulti()) -> p2sh, and returns the recognized scriptType on the config. Importing a p2sh or p2sh-p2wsh descriptor therefore creates a multisig stored as p2wsh — every derived address is wrong, funds appear missing. Fix: use config.scriptType from parseDescriptor. Found while implementing cairn-1kc3.1 (the new 48' suffix-vs-scriptType check would falsely reject correct sh(wsh()) descriptors because of this).
close_reason: Wizard half fixed (API half was already fixed earlier on this branch): src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/+page.server.ts import action now uses config.scriptType ?? 'p2wsh' from parseDescriptor instead of the hardcoded 'p2wsh', stale comment removed, and validateMultisigKeyPaths(config) added at the boundary to match the API route. sh(wsh(sortedmulti())) and sh(sortedmulti()) descriptors now prefill the wizard with the real script type (p2sh-p2wsh / p2sh) instead of a confusing suffix-mismatch rejection at create. check + full suite green.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: API half FIXED in this change: src/routes/api/wallets/multisig/import/+server.ts parseSource now uses config.scriptType from parseDescriptor (and runs validateMultisigKeyPaths on the parsed config). REMAINING (why this stays open): src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/+page.server.ts 'import' action (~line 225) has the identical hardcoded 'p2wsh' + stale comment — untouched here because the wizard's server action is shared surface with cairn-fdlf (Wave-A scope boundary). Note the blast radius is now smaller: even via the wizard prefill, createMultisig's new suffix-vs-scriptType check (cairn-1kc3.1) rejects the mismatched creation instead of silently storing a wrong-script-type wallet — the remaining bug is a confusing rejection, no longer silent corruption.

title: No educational tooltips anywhere in the explorer (block/tx/address jargon unexplained)
description:
Cairn is framed as an educational Bitcoin explorer, but block/tx/address detail pages surface raw protocol jargon with zero explanation: DIFFICULTY, NONCE, BITS, VERSION, WEIGHT, MERKLE ROOT on block pages; LOCKTIME on tx pages; address type codes like "P2WSH"/"P2WPKH" on address pages. None of these have hover tooltips, info icons, or inline help text.

Verified via code search: `grep -ri "tooltip|educational|help-text|info-icon"` across src/ returns zero matches — there is no tooltip infrastructure in the codebase at all.

Repro: visit /explorer/block/<any height> and hover over "DIFFICULTY", "NONCE", "BITS" labels — nothing happens. Same for address type badge on /explorer/address/<addr>.

Impact: for an app whose differentiator is explaining Bitcoin to newcomers, this is a significant gap versus the stated goal, not just a nice-to-have.
close_reason: Fixed in 0046d11: Term tooltip component with dotted-underline affordance now explains nonce, difficulty, bits, merkle root, weight, fees, vsize, locktime, RBF, SegWit, address types, and more across block/tx/address pages, plus collapsible How-does-this-work panels per page

title: Add-wallet wizard: hardware wallet option buttons (Trezor/Ledger/ColdCard/etc.) have no accessible name
description: On step 2 of Add a wallet (/wallets/new), the 7 key-source option cards (Trezor, Ledger, ColdCard, BitBox02, Jade, Air-gapped QR, Paste public key) render as <button> elements with no accessible name -- reading the page's accessibility tree shows them only as generic 'button' with no label. A screen-reader user would hear 'button, button, button...' with no way to tell them apart. Likely missing aria-label or the visible heading text isn't associated with the button role.
close_reason: Fixed in 22ab66d: the 7 key-source method cards now carry an explicit aria-label={title} so screen readers announce each by name (wallets/new/+page.svelte).

title: Multisig wallet sharing (invite cosigner/viewer) has no UI entry point
description:
Reproduced on Heartwood v0.2.0 at umbrel.local:3211 (browser QA of collaborative custody, item 4 of the QA pass).

Contacts work end-to-end: sent a request from a second test account (qa-second@cairn.local) to admin@cairn.local, accepted it from the admin side, "QA Second User" now shows under Settings > Contacts > Your contacts with a Remove action. That part is solid.

But there is no way to actually *use* a contact to share a multisig wallet. The backend is fully built:
  - GET/POST /api/wallets/multisig/[id]/shares (src/routes/api/wallets/multisig/[id]/shares/+server.ts) -- list collaborators / share with a contact as 'viewer' or 'cosigner', optionally scoped to specific keyIds
  - DELETE presumably on /shares/[shareId] (src/routes/api/wallets/multisig/[id]/shares/[shareId]/+server.ts)
  - src/lib/server/multisigShares.ts (shareMultisig, listCollaborators, ShareError) with its own test file
  - multisigAccess.test.ts, contacts.test.ts also cover pieces of this

grep across src for shareMultisig/listCollaborators/ShareRole turns up zero .svelte files -- nothing in the multisig wallet detail page (src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/[id]/+page.svelte), the wallets list, or anywhere else calls these endpoints. A wallet owner has no button, no modal, nothing that says "share this wallet" or "add a cosigner" anywhere in the UI. The Contacts page itself only manages the contact relationship ("start sharing a multisig wallet with them" is literally in its copy) but never links anywhere that lets you follow through.

This is the core mechanic of "collaborative custody" (per docs/COLLABORATIVE-CUSTODY plan and the 3-tier access gate already wired into routes) and right now it's unreachable -- a user can build a shared multisig wallet in the wizard (which does have "Shared with other people" as a vault-type option) but can never actually invite the other keyholders as cosigners/viewers through the app.

Suggested fix: add a "Collaborators" section to the multisig wallet detail page (parallel to the existing Keys/Export config sections) that lists current collaborators via GET /shares and lets the owner pick a contact + role (viewer/cosigner) + optional key scope via POST /shares, with remove via DELETE /shares/[shareId].
close_reason: Fixed in f757a19, merged to single-sig-full-wallet (62ba92b window). Added a Collaborators section to the multisig wallet detail page (parallel to Keys/Export): lists current collaborators + roles + assigned keys via GET /shares, lets the owner add a contact with a role (viewer/cosigner) + optional key scope via POST /shares, revoke via DELETE /shares/[shareId]. Gated on role === 'owner' && instanceMode === 'team', reusing the access role already computed in +page.server.ts. New component: src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/_components/MultisigCollaborators.svelte. npm run check 0/0; 28 backend tests (multisigShares/contacts/multisigAccess) unaffected and green.

title: No LICENSE file in public repo
description: Cairn is a public open-source repo but has no LICENSE file. Task spec requires MIT license from commit one. Add a standard MIT LICENSE file at the repo root with the correct copyright holder/year.
close_reason: Fixed: MIT LICENSE added at repo root in cb72bcc

title: Wallet detail balance chart has no axes, labels, or hover tooltips
description:
Alex's screenshot feedback on the wallet detail page's balance chart (src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/_components/WalletStepChart.svelte, rendered at src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte:550 under the 'balance over time · each step is a transaction' caption).

Current implementation is a bare, hand-rolled SVG stepped-line chart: a path + gradient fill + pulsing end-dot, marked aria-hidden=true, with zero axis rendering, zero labels, and zero interactivity -- it computes x/y geometry from the tx-delta series internally but never surfaces the underlying time/value scale to the viewer. The chart has the right data, just no presentation of it. Reported gaps, compared to mempool.space / typical financial app charts:

1. No Y-axis labels -- no BTC/sats scale marked on the vertical axis, so a viewer can't tell what the chart's height range represents.
2. No X-axis labels -- no dates/time period markers, so a viewer can't tell what timespan the chart covers.
3. No hover tooltips -- no way to see the exact balance + date at any point on the line; the only readable value today is the current confirmed balance shown elsewhere on the page.
4. The step line floats with no surrounding context (no baseline reference beyond the gradient fill sitting on the chart's own bottom edge).
5. The 'each step is a transaction' caption describes the interaction model well, but the chart itself doesn't label which step corresponds to which transaction/date.

Suggested fix direction: extend WalletStepChart.svelte (component is entirely self-contained: computes its own x(t)/y(v) scale functions locally, see geometry .by around line 51) to render:
- Y-axis: 2-4 horizontal gridlines with sats/BTC value labels (reuse formatSats/formatBtc from /format, matching the value formatting already used elsewhere on this page).
- X-axis: a handful of date labels along the bottom (reuse timeAgo or a short date formatter from /format for consistency), spaced to avoid crowding on narrow/mobile widths.
- Hover/tap tooltip: on pointer move over the SVG, find the nearest series point (via the existing x(t) mapping) and show its date + balance in a small tooltip, with a marker dot/vertical guideline at that x position. Needs to work on both mouse hover and touch tap for mobile.
- Optional: subtle horizontal gridlines at the Y-axis label positions (light var(--border-subtle) or var(--hairline) strokes), matching the Heartwood design system's existing hairline grammar rather than inventing a new grid style.

Check whether the dashboard's own BalanceChart.svelte (src/lib/components/portfolio/BalanceChart.svelte -- a different, smoothed-line chart per its own header comment, 'owned by a different lane') has any of this already solved that could be reused/adapted, since both charts likely need axes+tooltip eventually and duplicating the interaction logic twice would be wasteful. This bead scopes WalletStepChart only; if BalanceChart has the same gaps, file a separate bead rather than expanding scope here.

Keep the existing 'unboxed, bleeds to content width, no frame' Heartwood aesthetic -- axes/tooltips should feel like a refinement of this chart's spirit, not a generic charting-library look-and-feel.
close_reason: Fixed in 69a609f, merged to single-sig-full-wallet. Added Y-axis gridlines + BTC labels (up to 3 rows spanning the chart's value range), X-axis date ticks (up to 4, time-spaced, snapped to real data points, skip-repeated-label guard), and a pointer/tap tooltip with a vertical guideline showing exact balance + date at any transaction step. Ported the interaction patterns from Home's BalanceChart.svelte (nearest-point-by-x lookup, HTML-overlay labels for crisp text) adapted to WalletStepChart's own pixel-space geometry and stepped visual style -- gradient fill, draw-in animation, and pulsing end-dot unchanged. Swapped aria-hidden for role=img + descriptive aria-label. npm run check 0/0, full suite 1682 passed/1 skipped. Live-verified in a real browser via a temporary scratch route (synthetic tx data, deleted after verification, never committed): gridlines/labels/ticks render correctly after mount, pointermove hover shows the right balance+date tooltip, pointerdown (tap) surfaces it too, pointerleave dismisses it correctly.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Checked: src/lib/components/portfolio/BalanceChart.svelte (the dashboard's chart) already solves this exact problem -- it has X-axis date ticks (~line 137-159), Y-axis min/max labels from honest data extremes (~line 191), a pointermove-driven hover tooltip with nearest-point lookup (~line 205-243), and a vertical guideline + halo dot at the hover point (~line 354-362). WalletStepChart should adapt this same pattern rather than reinvent it -- significantly lower scope than building axes/tooltips from scratch. The two components have different visual styles (BalanceChart is a smoothed line, WalletStepChart is a stepped line with a gradient fill), so this is porting the axis/tooltip INTERACTION logic, not copying the component wholesale.

title: Merge Ledger signer pair (LedgerSigner/MultisigLedgerSigner + StatelessLedgerSigner) into one component
description: Follow the merged Trezor pattern (src/lib/components/signing/TrezorSigner.svelte, multisig?: MultisigContext prop). LedgerSigner.svelte (339 lines) vs MultisigLedgerSigner.svelte (666) — the multisig variant adds the on-device BIP-388 wallet-policy registration flow (ledger-registration API) and wrong-device probing; StatelessLedgerSigner.svelte (wallets/multisig/stateless/_components) duplicates much of it and should fold in too. Signing UI: preserve all copy/flows/error handling exactly; verify with svelte-check + vitest + loading both send pages in preview. Ideally hardware-test before shipping.
close_reason: Merged into src/lib/components/signing/LedgerSigner.svelte (multisig?: MultisigContext with optional multisigId for persistent vs stateless registration); StatelessLedgerSigner folded in; all 3 call sites updated; svelte-check 0 errors, 1120 vitest passing

title: Extend admin-leak marker sweep to chain-backed routes
description: src/tests/adminLeakAudit.test.ts (added by cairn-f5gh, track/adminleak @ d55beea, merged 13caa65) deliberately skips /api/wallets, /api/portfolio, and explorer routes in its structural marker-diff sweep because they need live chain-service mocks (Electrum/Core RPC) to load without erroring. This pass manually reviewed those routes and found them clean, but there's no standing regression gate over them the way there is for the rest of the app. Follow-up: build chain-service mocks (Electrum/Core RPC responses) sufficient to exercise these routes' load functions under the same non-admin-session marker-diff technique, and fold them into the existing adminLeakAudit.test.ts sweep so future admin-data leaks into these routes are caught automatically.
