title: Harden non-admin (app) form actions with explicit requireUser — the systemic root cause
description: Root cause (Fable, review-2026-07-07): SvelteKit form actions never run a parent layout load(), so the (app)/+layout.server.ts LOGIN redirect also never gates actions — only the locals.user!.id deref (masked 500) saves the non-admin (app)/* actions today. This is defense-by-accident. Do ONE mechanical sweep adding requireUser(event) as the first line of every (app)/* form action (wallets/*, settings/*, etc.), converting masked-500 -> clean 401 and hardening against a future refactor flipping a ! to ?.. Two anon-reachable actions confirmed to use optional chaining (harmless today, no mutation): wallets/multisig/new key/preview and wallets/new preview. Keep separate from the P0 auth fix so it can't destabilize money-path files. Optionally also evaluate a hooks.server.ts authenticated-non-GET guard for all protected routes (needs a public-POST allowlist: login/signup/logout/agreement/disclosure/setup-admin) — larger change, real false-positive risk, scope carefully.

title: Auth: fetch()-based login returns 200 but session cookie not persisted (/api/auth/me 401 immediately after)
description: Reproduced 2026-07-12 during browser QA on dev instances (:5311 and default dev server). Raw fetch POST to /api/auth/login/password returns 200 + user object, but a follow-up GET /api/auth/me returns 401 immediately after. Suspect Set-Cookie attributes (Secure/SameSite) vs plain-http localhost/preview proxy. UI form-based login also silently no-ops pre-d1cb050. Server/auth domain — NOT for the dev/UX session to fix.
close_reason: Fixed: server.mjs defaults PROTOCOL_HEADER=x-forwarded-proto for unconfigured deployments + scripts/serverProto.mjs fill-when-absent helper on both listeners. Curl-verified against the real built server: plain-HTTP login no longer sets Secure (session now persists); inbound x-forwarded-proto:https still honored (Secure preserved for real reverse-proxy topologies); CSRF-origin check also confirmed correct in both directions. See bead comment for full root cause, curl matrix, and sec sign-off. Tests: src/tests/serverProto.test.ts (5/5), auth.test.ts cookieSecure block unchanged/green, npm run check 0 errors.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE (confirmed 2026-07-13, server-side): adapter-node's `get_origin()` treats
every request as https whenever NEITHER `ORIGIN` NOR `PROTOCOL_HEADER` is configured
(build/handler.js, the compiled adapter-node runtime — see the SvelteKit adapter-node
source around the same logic, `~208-211` in the built handler for this version). Cairn's
server.mjs (bare-node entry point, no reverse proxy) never set either env var, so on an
unconfigured/direct deployment EVERY request — including ones that hit the plain HTTP
listener — "looked" https to adapter-node. src/lib/server/auth.ts:130-140 `cookieSecure()`
follows that (derived) request protocol unless CAIRN_ORIGIN explicitly declares plain
http, so the session cookie got `Secure` stamped on a plain-HTTP response. Browsers
silently drop a `Secure` cookie set over plain HTTP — login returns 200 with a user
object, but the cookie never sticks, so the next request looks unauthenticated. This is
exactly the fetch-200-but-/api/auth/me-401 symptom in the bead title.

EMPIRICAL CURL MATRIX (built prod bundle, `node server.mjs` on a spare port + scratch
DB, no ORIGIN/PROTOCOL_HEADER set — reproducing a bare unproxied deployment):
  - BEFORE the fix: POST /api/auth/login/password over plain http, no
    x-forwarded-proto header -> Set-Cookie carried `Secure` (bug reproduced).
  - AFTER the fix, same request -> `set-cookie: cairn_session=...; Path=/; ...;
    HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax` — NO Secure attribute.
  - AFTER the fix, same request but with an injected `x-forwarded-proto: https`
    header -> `set-cookie: ...; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax` (fill-when-absent
    correctly honors an inbound reverse-proxy header instead of clobbering it).
  - Bonus verification (CSRF-origin, same server): POST to a real form action
    (/setup-admin?/complete) with a matching `Origin: http://127.0.0.1:<port>` ->
    200/redirect (succeeds); same POST with a cross-scheme `Origin: https://...` ->
    403 "Cross-site POST form submissions are forbidden". Confirms SvelteKit's
    built-in CSRF-origin check now derives the correct http origin for the plain
    listener too (it was ALSO broken by the same https-default before this fix,
    though that direction fails closed rather than silently).

FIX (fill-when-absent, not an unconditional overwrite):
  1. server.mjs now defaults `PROTOCOL_HEADER=x-forwarded-proto` when the operator
     has set neither `ORIGIN` nor `PROTOCOL_HEADER` (respects an explicit operator
     choice either way).
  2. New pure helper `scripts/serverProto.mjs` `fillForwardedProto(headers, proto)` —
     sets `x-forwarded-proto` to the listener's own protocol ONLY when the header is
     absent; never overwrites an inbound value. Wired into both server.mjs listener
     wrappers (http fills 'http', https fills 'https').
  3. Dockerfile updated: runtime image now also copies scripts/serverProto.mjs
     (server.mjs's new dependency), plus a doc note next to the existing
     ADDRESS_HEADER note explaining the new default and that a TLS-terminating
     reverse proxy in front of the HTTP port MUST set its own X-Forwarded-Proto
     (or set ORIGIN/CAIRN_ORIGIN) since the fill never overwrites.
  4. src/lib/server/auth.ts cookieSecure(), webauthn.ts, and
     packaging/umbrel/heartwood/docker-compose.yml deliberately left untouched —
     Umbrel already sets its own PROTOCOL_HEADER=x-forwarded-proto via app_proxy,
     so this fix targets exactly the bare/unproxied deployment gap cookieSecure()
     couldn't see.

TEST EVIDENCE:
  - New unit tests, src/tests/serverProto.test.ts (5 tests): fills 'http' when
    absent, PRESERVES an inbound x-forwarded-proto: https (regression guard for
    reverse-proxy topologies), fills 'https' on the https listener, never mutates
    other headers, returns the same object.
  - src/lib/server/auth.test.ts `cookieSecure` describe block: unchanged, still
    green (6 tests) — this fix does not touch cookieSecure() itself, only what
    protocol the request URL carries by the time cookieSecure() sees it.
  - `npm run check`: 0 errors, 0 warnings (1883 files).
  - Runtime curl matrix above, against the actual built `build/handler.js` via
    `node server.mjs`.

SEC SIGN-OFF (explicit, per review request):
  1. Fill-when-absent spoofing residual is self-inflicted-only: on the HTTP
     listener, a client that sends its OWN `x-forwarded-proto: https` gets a
     `Secure` cookie stamped on a plain-HTTP response — but that cookie is then
     silently dropped by THAT SAME client's own browser (Secure cookies are never
     set over a non-TLS connection per browser cookie rules). There is no cross-user
     or cross-origin impact; the only party who can trigger it is also the only
     party who "loses" (their own login stops sticking). Accepted.
  2. Accepted loud regression for TLS-terminating proxies that omit
     X-Forwarded-Proto in front of the plain HTTP port: such a deployment now gets
     an INCORRECTLY-NON-Secure cookie over what the proxy presents to the browser
     as https (rather than the previous default of incorrectly-Secure-over-http).
     This is a "loud" failure (cookie still transmitted, just missing a hardening
     attribute) rather than the previous "silent total auth breakage" failure, and
     is documented as a hard requirement in the Dockerfile and this bead. No known
     Cairn deployment topology (Umbrel's app_proxy, the primary target) hits this,
     since it already sets its own X-Forwarded-Proto.
  3. Trust model: the HTTP listener now honors an inbound `x-forwarded-proto`
     header from ANY client, not just a configured trusted proxy (there is no
     source-IP or shared-secret gate on it — matching adapter-node's own
     PROTOCOL_HEADER trust model, which has no such gate either). This is the same
     trust boundary Cairn already accepts for ADDRESS_HEADER (x-forwarded-for,
     Dockerfile comment) and ADDRESS_HEADER/HOST_HEADER in Umbrel's compose file.
     Given finding (1) above, honoring it on the unauthenticated pre-session path
     has no exploitable effect beyond the requesting client's own cookie.

Files: server.mjs, scripts/serverProto.mjs (new), src/tests/serverProto.test.ts (new),
Dockerfile.

title: Critical auth routes (register/recover/login verify) have zero test coverage
description: Four highest-security auth route handlers are completely untested: (1) api/auth/register/verify — admin-reclaim rejection guard + atomic account creation, (2) api/auth/recover/verify — anti-enumeration constant-time contract, (3) api/auth/login/password — break-glass admin backdoor wiring + disabled/bad mapping, (4) api/auth/login/verify — cross-user passkey-challenge binding check. Also api/admin/restore (highest-blast-radius admin op). Fix: create auth route tests using a shared RequestEvent factory, pin each security property.

file that ships this app (heartwood-app-store/heartwood-bitcoin,
packaging/umbrel/heartwood in this repo, AND the currently-installed
app-data compose on umbrel-s15). umbrelProbe.ts hard-gates on

fire, which would have shipped 0.2.16 exactly as broken as 0.2.15 was.

packaging mirror as part of this release (see commits below).

PROBE VERIFICATION (umbrel-s15, localhost:2000, real umbreld daemon +
nested Docker — not a bare mock):

   umbrel-s15 docker load` — loaded clean.

   backed up first) to point `web.image` at the local 0.2.16 tag and add
   `CAIRN_PLATFORM: umbrel`.

   Node `net` server answering `blockchain.headers.subscribe` with a
   fake header) as a container on `umbrel_main_network` at the fixed IP
   10.21.21.10:50001 (electrs's well-known slot), reusing the already-
   cached 0.2.15 image (has node) as the runtime so no extra pull was
   needed. Wiped cairn.db* for a fresh install, restarted the app via
   umbreld's own `legacy-compat/app-script restart heartwood-bitcoin`
   (same path umbreld itself uses — not a raw `docker compose up`).
   Log evidence (heartwood-bitcoin_web_1):
     {"tag":"umbrel-probe","event":"umbrel_probe_connected","host":"10.21.21.10","port":50001,"label":"electrs","msg":"auto-connected to Umbrel Electrum via credential-free probe"}
     {"tag":"startup","version":"0.2.16",...,"chain":{"electrumHost":"10.21.21.10","electrumPort":50001,"electrumTls":false,...},"seededThisBoot":true,...}
   Direct DB read of the settings table confirmed persistence:

   IP. Log evidence:
     {"tag":"chain","consecutiveFailures":2,"lastError":"Electrum connect to 10.21.21.200:50002 timed out after 2000ms","msg":"chain transport unhealthy: too many consecutive connection failures"}
     {"tag":"startup","version":"0.2.16",...,"chain":{"electrumHost":"electrum.blockstream.info","electrumPort":50002,"electrumTls":true,...},"seededThisBoot":false,...}
   No `umbrel_probe_connected` event fired; settings table query for
   connection_mode/electrum_host/chain_provisioned_by returned zero rows
   (never written) — the public-server default is silently in effect,
   exactly as designed. App booted clean and healthy:
   `curl -sk https://127.0.0.1:5588/api/health` -> `{"status":"ok"}`
   HTTP 200, container status "Up ... (healthy)".
5. Restored umbrel-s15 to its original 0.2.15 compose + original cairn.db
   afterward (shared QA container used by other in-flight beads) and
   confirmed it came back up healthy.

PUBLISH: `docker push ghcr.io/alexm223/cairn:0.2.16` blocked —
`error from registry: permission_denied: The token provided does not
match expected scopes.` No login was attempted (per instructions); the

pin the real digest and are pushed, and a live umbreld-managed update from 0.2.15 -> 0.2.16 was exercised

title: Vault signing stepper: all keys shown as 'signed' when fingerprints collide, blocking the 2nd signature
description:
Page: /vaults/[id]/send (Sign step)

What I did: created a 2-of-3 vault via the wizard using three keys derived
from the SAME seed at different BIP-48 accounts (m/48'/0'/0'/2', m/48'/0'/1'/2',
m/48'/0'/2'/2') -- all three therefore share the same master fingerprint
(73c5da0a). The wizard let this through with no warning (see cairn-<duplicate
fingerprint bead, filed alongside this one>). Built a send, went to Sign,
downloaded the unsigned PSBT, signed it for real with Test Key 1's actual
private key (verified: the resulting PSBT has exactly 1 partialSig and all 3
original bip32Derivation entries -- confirmed via direct PSBT inspection, not
just the UI), and attached it via "paste base64/hex".

What happened: the banner correctly reads "1 of 2 signatures collected · 1
more signature needed" and the header correctly says "Key 2 of 2" -- the
underlying progress COUNT is right. But all three key chips (Test Key 1,
Test Key 2, AND Test Key 3) render with the "signed" CSS class and a green
checkmark, including the two keys that have never touched this PSBT. Worse:
with all three visually marked "signed," no "Sign with Test Key 2 — file"
panel renders at all -- the page just shows the key list, the success toast
for Key 1, and a "Back to review" button. There is no way to proceed and add
the required second signature. Reproduced twice, including after a full page
reload (ruling out a transient render glitch).

Root cause (hypothesis, worth confirming against the actual code): the
"has this key signed" check most likely matches by fingerprint rather than by
the specific pubkey tied to each key's own bip32Derivation entry. All three
of this vault's keys share fingerprint 73c5da0a, so once ANY partialSig from
that fingerprint exists, the check likely (incorrectly) treats every key
sharing that fingerprint as satisfied, even though only one of the three
distinct pubkeys actually has a signature attached.

Why this matters even though it's an edge case: the vault wizard's own
missing duplicate-fingerprint warning (filed separately) makes it easy for a
user to end up in exactly this state -- e.g., someone who doesn't realize
"the recovery key" they pasted was derived from the same seed as their
hardware wallet's key. When that happens, this bug makes the vault
completely UNUSABLE for spending: there's no way to add the second signature
through this UI, meaning funds already sent to the vault would be stuck
(without falling back to raw PSBT tools outside Cairn entirely).

What should happen: the "signed" check should match the exact pubkey used in
each partialSig against the exact pubkey in each key's own bip32Derivation
entry (not just the shared fingerprint), so only the key that actually
contributed a signature is marked "signed," and the Sign step correctly
presents the panel for the next real signer.

Priority: P1 -- this can leave a real vault completely stuck (no path to
finish signing through the UI) whenever two of its keys happen to share a
fingerprint, which the wizard doesn't currently prevent or warn about.
close_reason: Fixed and live-reproduced: per-key pubkey attribution in multisigPsbtProgress (keys[] with fingerprint+origin-path identity), UI matches identity, finalization-strip case degrades honestly with a quorum-met banner. 3-test regression block incl. the exact tester scenario. signedFingerprints kept for existing consumers.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Fixed. Root cause confirmed: multisigPsbtProgress attributed by fingerprint only (signedFingerprints) and the send page's isSigned() matched key.fingerprint against that set. Fix: progress module now emits per-key attribution (progress.keys: {fingerprint, path, signed}) by matching each partialSig pubkey against each key's own bip32Derivation entry, identity = fingerprint + account-origin path; UI matches roster keys on that identity. signedFingerprints kept for existing consumers. Finalization-strip handled: complete PSBTs show quorum-met state (no false all-signed, no signer panel, Continue-to-broadcast CTA). Regression tests: 2-of-3 all sharing fp 73c5da0a - sign key 1 -> exactly key 1 attributed, next-signer = key 2; finalized -> no fabricated attribution. Live-verified on the dev stack (vault 'x54 Collision Repro 2-of-3' id 7, tx 14): after key 1's real signature the page shows '1 of 2 signatures collected', one signed chip, and the 'Sign with Test Key 2 - file' panel renders; key 2's signature completes the quorum. NOTE: the stateless signing page (wallets/multisig/stateless/+page.svelte:273) still matches by bare fingerprint - same bug class, outside this fix's scope.

Only the verifyAuthentication throw path is tested. A regression here is a silent auth-bypass class. title: No password recovery / forgot-password flow
description: Confirmed via grep: no forgot-password or password-reset route exists anywhere in src/routes. For a self-hosted single-admin app with no configured mail server, a locked-out admin has no recovery path short of direct database surgery. At minimum this deserves a documented recovery procedure (README section: how to reset a password via the CLI/DB if you're locked out); a proper in-app flow would need an SMTP config the admin sets up, which may be out of scope for v1 given the added complexity (mail server configuration) for a niche case. Recommend: ship the documented manual-recovery procedure now (cheap), consider an in-app flow later if self-hosters ask for it.

title: Collaborative custody: shared wallets invisible/inaccessible to collaborators — sharing entirely disconnected from access routes
description:
Full E2E test performed 2026-07-05/06 as user hwtest-b@example.com and hwtest-c@example.com (real accounts, real invite-code signup) against a real 2-of-3 multisig wallet (id 3) owned by the admin. Confirmed via live HTTP requests, not just code reading:

- Admin successfully shared the wallet with both users as 'cosigner' role with assigned keys via POST /api/wallets/multisig/3/shares — this succeeded and multisig_shares rows + multisig_keys.assigned_user_id were correctly written (owner's GET .../shares correctly lists both collaborators with their assigned key IDs).
- User B's GET /api/wallets/multisig (the route backing the wallet list page) returns {"multisigs":[],"errors":{}} — the shared wallet never appears. Root cause: src/lib/server/multisigShares.ts's listSharedMultisigs() is never imported/called anywhere in src/routes — confirmed via repo-wide grep, zero call sites outside its own definition.
- User B's GET /api/wallets/multisig/3 (direct-by-ID access) returns 404 "Multisig not found". Root cause: src/routes/api/wallets/multisig/[id]/+server.ts calls getMultisig(user.id, id) (owner-only lookup), never getViewableMultisig or getSignableMultisig (both defined in src/lib/server/wallets/multisig.ts:147/167 but — confirmed via repo-wide grep — have ZERO call sites anywhere outside their own definitions).
- Also zero call sites anywhere: multisigAccessRole() and redactMultisigKeysForViewer() (both in multisigShares.ts) — the entire 3-tier owner/cosigner/viewer access-gate layer described in docs/COLLABORATIVE-CUSTODY-PLAN.md is built but not wired into a single route.

Impact: collaborative custody, the flagship multi-user feature, is 100% non-functional end-to-end despite complete backend plumbing (contacts, shares, key assignment, redaction all work correctly in isolation). A collaborator who has been shared a wallet has no way to discover it, view it, or sign from it via any route. This blocks the multi-user case of the requested hardware-wallet E2E co-signing test entirely — signing tests proceed under the single owning admin account instead as a fallback, since real cross-user co-signing cannot currently be exercised.

Recommended fix: wire getViewableMultisig/getSignableMultisig into GET /api/wallets/multisig/[id] (falling back from getMultisig when the owner-only lookup misses), call listSharedMultisigs() in GET /api/wallets/multisig (merge with owned summaries, tag with role), and apply redactMultisigKeysForViewer() for the 'viewer' role case. Also wire multisigAccessRole() into whatever route currently gates PSBT signing/building so a cosigner can actually reach the sign step.
close_reason: Wired the 3-tier access gates + roster into every multisig route in commit 297ba2d. Cosigners/viewers now see shared wallets in the list (tagged 'Shared by X'), reach them by id, and cosigners can sign (roster-gated); pure viewers are read-only; non-participants get uniform 404. Regression test in multisigAccess.test.ts. check + 962 tests green.

title: Rapid navigation stutters/appears blocked — superseded loads aren't cancelled, sync SQLite serializes on the event loop
description:
## Reported symptoms (both are the same underlying bug)

1. "Navigation blocks during initial load" — clicking a nav item before the current page finishes loading appears to do nothing until data partially arrives.
2. "Rapid tab switching causes lag" — Dashboard -> Explorer -> Wallets clicked quickly queues up and the UI stutters through each load sequentially instead of the last click winning immediately.

Investigated both. There is NO code that blocks/covers nav-rail clicks (see "ruled out" below) — what actually happens is worse: the click DOES fire a real navigation, but the new page's data genuinely queues behind the abandoned old page's still-running server work, which reads as "nothing happened" / "stutters through each load."

## Root cause

**SvelteKit's client router discards a superseded navigation's *result* via token comparison, but never aborts the *request*.** `node_modules/@sveltejs/kit/src/runtime/client/client.js` `load_data` (~line 3110-3123) does `await fetcher(data_url.href, {})` with no `AbortController`/`signal`. None of our load functions accept or check a cancellation signal either — `src/routes/(app)/+page.server.ts`, `explorer/+page.server.ts`, `wallets/+page.server.ts`, `+layout.server.ts` all destructure only `{locals, depends, parent, url}`.

**The abandoned server-side work is synchronous and blocks Node's single event-loop thread.** `src/lib/server/db.ts:21` — `export const db = new DatabaseSync(DB_PATH)` — this is `node:sqlite`'s *synchronous* API. `+layout.server.ts` runs on every navigation and does several sequential sync `db.prepare().get()/.all()` calls (`mustResetPassword`, `hasAcceptedAdminDisclosure`/`hasAcceptedCurrentAgreement`, `hasRecoverySetup`, `isFirstSyncComplete`, `listUnbackedWallets`, `shouldShowBackupReminder`, `listActiveAnnouncementsFor`). Dashboard's `+page.server.ts` adds its own sync `hasWallets` query. Because Node is single-threaded, three rapid-fire navigations (Dashboard->Explorer->Wallets) each trigger this synchronous chain and they literally **serialize on the event loop** — the second request's JS cannot begin executing until the first (already-abandoned) request's synchronous SQLite work finishes.

**Electrum requests add secondary backpressure.** `src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts`'s `pending` map is only cleared on response/15s-timeout/disconnect — nothing removes a request when its caller navigates away, so an abandoned load keeps one of only `DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE = 2` (`pool.ts:30`) connections busy via round-robin, starving the new navigation's own Electrum calls.

## Ruled out (so the fix stays focused)

No global loading overlay, no `pointer-events: none`, no `$navigating`/`beforeNavigate` gating, no view-transitions config (no `svelte.config.js`/`hooks.client.ts` exists at all) — the nav rail (`HWRail.svelte`) lives in the persistent `(app)/+layout.svelte`, outside `{@render children()}`, with plain `<a href>` links, so it is never torn down or disabled by navigation/streaming. Don't waste time looking for a "blocked nav" fix distinct from this one.

## Fix

1. Reduce/batch the synchronous SQLite calls in `+layout.server.ts` — several independent `db.prepare().get()` round-trips that run on literally every navigation are the biggest lever, since that file's load always runs regardless of destination route. Combine into fewer queries where possible.
2. Thread an abort signal through to the Electrum pool/client so a superseded request's connection is released immediately instead of occupying one of the 2 pooled slots for up to 15s.
3. Consider whether `+layout.server.ts`'s per-navigation checks (backup reminders, announcements, agreement status) actually need to re-run every single navigation vs. once per session/TTL — several of these are user-account-state, not chain-state, and don't need `depends('cairn:chain')`-style freshness.
close_reason: done

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Partial fix landed via commit f0dd421 (worktree agent-a3a6100930742990e). Discovered the bead's original root-cause writeup was partially stale: cairn-v84z/isda (3dd2b4a) already moved the 4 access gates out of the layout load and made it dependency-free, so SvelteKit already skips re-invoking it on most client navs -- the biggest lever was already pulled. This pass reduced the remaining second-tier round-trips: new getInstanceMode() (single keyed SELECT replacing a full settings-table scan + secret decrypt), new getUserAgreementOperator() (one query replacing 3), and combined shouldShowBackupReminder()'s two sequential queries into one. NOT attempted (per bead's own conservative guidance, left for a future pass): relaxing per-nav freshness on backup-reminder/announcement checks, and Electrum pool abort-signal threading for superseded requests. Leaving OPEN -- not fully resolved.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Fable-scoped follow-up (2026-07-08, mandatory-consult workflow): audited every page route directly -- the cairn-vknb streaming pass already covers every page-level chain call in the app (dashboard, wallets list, wallet detail, send, multisig detail, admin, all explorer pages). The bead's original 'page-level Electrum calls still block on ARM' premise has drifted stale: the actual event-loop-blocking mechanism was sync SQLite (fixed by f0dd421 + cairn-t72a), not awaited Electrum/esplora I/O, which never blocks the event loop or other users -- it only delays TTFB of the one request awaiting it. Pool size (2) and the 15s connect timeout were both checked and are non-issues (round-robin dispatch has no checkout/exhaustion; abandoned requests don't head-of-line-block new writes). Two small, real items remain, scoped as cairn-y2kf.

Nothing further scoped.

title: Admin Overview 'Wallets' count omits multisig wallets
description:
Repro: as admin, imported 1 single-sig wallet and 1 multisig wallet (2 wallets total for the account). Admin > Overview shows "WALLETS: 1 · imported across all users" — undercounts by 1.

Root cause: src/lib/server/admin.ts:200, instanceStats() computes `wallets` as `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM wallets` only — it never counts the `multisigs` table.

This is inconsistent with listUsers() in the same file (lines 10-12), which correctly sums both:
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM wallets w WHERE w.user_id = u.id) + (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM multisigs m WHERE m.user_id = u.id) AS wallet_count
So the Admin > Users page shows the right per-user count while the Admin > Overview page's instance-wide total is wrong.

Fix: instanceStats() should add `(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM multisigs)` to the wallets count, mirroring listUsers()'s approach.
close_reason: Fixed in c110211

title: Portfolio scans failing/extremely slow due to Tor SOCKS5 proxy rejecting connections
description: On the Umbrel instance (v0.2.0), admin > Settings has a SOCKS5/Tor proxy configured (192.168.50.134:9050) for chain traffic. That proxy is currently rejecting connections ('SocksClientError: Socks5 proxy rejected connection - Failure' per admin > Logs), which causes: (1) /api/portfolio scan warnings 'portfolio scan failed; wallet excluded from totals' for all 4 wallets simultaneously, repeated at 12:21, 13:33, and 14:10 in today's logs; (2) /api/portfolio requests that DO succeed taking 16s-135s (observed: 16981ms, 37964ms, 107223ms, 135080ms) instead of failing fast; (3) user-visible effect during QA: dashboard total-balance skeleton stuck loading for 8-10+ seconds on first login, and one /wallets navigation appeared to hang for 7+ seconds. Two asks: (a) Alex should check whether the Tor daemon at 192.168.50.134:9050 is actually up -- this may just be infra on his network being down; (b) regardless, Cairn's portfolio/wallet-scan path should fail fast and surface a clear 'can't reach chain backend' banner instead of hanging up to 135 seconds per request when the configured proxy is unreachable -- no request should be allowed to block that long. Also noted in admin > Activity: 'Bitcoin node connection down' errors and repeated fallback from local Electrum (192.168.50.146:5002) to the public electrum.blockstream.info server.
close_reason: Fixed in 62ba92b, same underlying mechanism as cairn-3gvb: the 10s per-wallet scan timeout means a rejecting/hanging SOCKS5 proxy no longer produces 16-135s portfolio responses -- every branch settles (success or excluded) within the fixed budget. No proxy-specific code path was needed; timeout and proxy-rejection both funnel through the same withTimeout race and per-wallet catch/log path. npm run check 0/0, full suite green.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: Follow-up: verified https://umbrel.local:3212/api/health directly via curl -k -- currently healthy, 200 OK in ~20ms, 5/5 attempts. The 503s seen in the browser network tab for this endpoint during QA were transient and correlate with the same load window as the portfolio-scan slowness (~14:14-14:16), not a standalone HTTPS-listener bug. title: Wallet/multisig detail pages check backup status via localStorage, not the server-tracked wallet_backups table
description:
Reproduced live: imported a single-sig wallet via the /wallets/new wizard, downloaded its config on the mandatory Step 5 ("Wallet imported — one important step left"). Server log confirmed `GET /api/wallets/1/config 200`, which calls markBackedUp() (src/lib/server/backups.ts) and persists a row in wallet_backups. Then navigated to the wallet's detail page (/wallets/1) — it still showed a "not downloaded yet" warning badge for the backup.

Root cause: src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte:144-153 computes `backupDone` purely from `localStorage.getItem('cairn.wallet.backup.{id}')`, set only by that same page's own download button onclick handler (line 477/485: markBackupDownloaded). It never calls the server-tracked isBackedUp() (src/lib/server/backups.ts:31), which the wizard's download endpoint and the persistent "back up your wallet" banner (src/routes/(app)/+layout.server.ts via listUnbackedWallets) both already rely on. The page's +page.server.ts load() (src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.server.ts) doesn't fetch or pass backup status at all.

Same bug pattern exists on the multisig detail page: src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/[id]/+page.svelte:42-48 (backupDone from localStorage key `cairn.multisig.backup.{id}`), used at lines 198 and 488.

Effect: two disconnected sources of truth for "has this wallet's backup been downloaded" — the persistent top banner is server-tracked and correct, but each wallet/multisig detail page's own on-page badge is client-only and will show "not downloaded yet" even after a real download (e.g. via the creation wizard, a different browser, or after clearing site data), or conversely could show "backed up" on a fresh browser profile if... actually no, it's optimistic-true by default (line 146: `$state(true)`) so a NEW session's localStorage miss still shows correctly initially then flips false once checked — but it will never flip TRUE from a download that happened elsewhere. This contradicts the "server-tracked" design explicitly called out in recent commits 7c9ab8b (mandatory server-tracked wallet-config backup) and f7848e4 (persistent server-tracked banner), and undermines the backup-safety epic cairn-m4e (backups are treated with seed-phrase gravity — the on-page status should not lie).

Fix: have the load functions for both detail pages call isBackedUp() and pass it down, replacing the localStorage-based `backupDone` state (or use it only as an optimistic instant-UI-feedback layer synced with, not instead of, the server value).
close_reason: Both detail pages' load() now pass server-tracked isBackedUp() (wallet_backups) as data.backedUp; badge uses it as source of truth with an optimistic local overlay. localStorage-only logic removed.

title: Caravan import doesn't reject duplicate cosigner xpubs until a later step
description: parseCaravanImport() in src/lib/server/multisigExport.ts (around lines 242-255) has no duplicate-xpub check, so a Caravan JSON with the same xpub listed twice as different cosigners parses successfully at import time. The duplicate is only caught later, in resolveMultisig() (multisig.ts:256-266, 'every cosigner must be distinct'), which runs during address derivation/descriptor generation -- i.e. one step further into the wizard than where the bad input was actually accepted. Low severity (the wallet is never actually created with duplicate keys, it's still caught), but worth moving the duplicate check into parseCaravanImport itself so the error surfaces immediately at the paste/upload step instead of after the user has already moved on to the next wizard step, matching the other validation cairn already does inline there (threshold vs key count).
close_reason: Fixed in c110211

comments.author: alexl
comments.text: A second independent audit confirmed this and added detail: parseCaravanImport's per-key validation (multisigExport.ts:242-255) also never calls parseXpub, so it silently accepts (a) garbage xpub-shaped strings, and (b) wrong-network keys -- e.g. a tpub pasted into a config whose top-level 'network' field says 'mainnet' passes through unchecked, since the network field is only checked as a JSON string against the xpub's own version-byte prefix is never decoded/verified. Same bounded severity as the duplicate-key case: caught later at Review/Create via resolveMultisig's real parseXpub call, so no wallet is ever created with a bad key, but the error surfaces later and less specifically than it should. Consider validating each key with parseXpub inline in parseCaravanImport for immediate, specific per-key error attribution (e.g. 'Key 2: this is a testnet key') instead of a generic downstream address-derivation failure.

title: Referrals: multisig wizard managed-service card
description: In the multisig creation wizard (src/routes/(app)/wallets/multisig/new/), render a small non-modal card below the DIY signer setup: 'Want a managed multisig service instead?' listing active multisig_service_referrals rows. Only render if >=1 active row exists and referral_links flag is on. Must not interrupt or block the DIY flow.

Full suite + svelte-check green.

This document will also double as a QA baseline.

title: NUL byte silently truncates stored text across all free-text write paths
description: Any string containing \x00 truncates at the NUL with no error, in createWallet name, setLabel, createMultisig name, addressLabels, addressBook, registerUser displayName. Likely one shared node:sqlite/V8 C-string marshaling cause. Silent data loss. Pinned in src/lib/server/hostileText.test.ts on branch test/qa-wave-2026-07-12, worktree C:\dev\cairn-testqa.

title: Settings back-button loop: in-app 'Back to settings' control pushes instead of popping history
description:
## Repro (reported by Alex, confirmed via code + live check)

1. Go to /settings.
2. Open a sub-page, e.g. API Tokens (/settings/tokens).
3. Tap the in-app "Back to settings" control at the top of the sub-page (chevron/arrow, visually looks like a real back button).
4. Press the browser's real back button once -> lands back on /settings/tokens (WRONG — should land on whatever page was open before Settings).
5. Press back again -> back to /settings. Every further "back" alternates between the two, an infinite loop.

## Root cause

`src/lib/components/heartwood/BackCircle.svelte`:
- No `href` (L15-24): `onclick={() => history.back()}` — a real back-navigation.
- With `href` set (L11-14): renders `<a {href}>` — a **forward-pushing** SvelteKit navigation, not a real back.

Every Settings sub-page's header calls it WITH an href, so it never does a real back:
- `src/routes/(app)/settings/tokens/+page.svelte:35` — `<BackCircle href="/settings" label="Back to settings" />`
- `src/routes/(app)/settings/devices/+page.svelte:30`, `settings/contacts/+page.svelte:115`, `settings/notifications/+page.svelte:511` — identical pattern.
- Desktop breadcrumbs do the same, e.g. `tokens/+page.svelte:41` — `<a class="crumb-link" href="/settings">`.

Confirmed live: the "Back to settings" element on /settings/tokens is an accessibility-tree `link` (`href="/settings"`), not a button wired to `history.back()`.

Stack after the repro: `[…Prior, /settings, /settings/tokens, /settings(new push from BackCircle)]`. First real browser-back pops to `/settings/tokens` (the entry directly below the fresh push) — not Prior. Second back pops to the original `/settings`. Every additional "Back to settings" tap re-inflates the stack with another `/settings <-> sub-page` pair, so the loop never resolves back to Prior.

No shared `+layout.svelte` exists under `src/routes/(app)/settings/` and there is zero `popstate`/`pushState`/`replaceState`/`beforeNavigate`/`afterNavigate` usage anywhere in the Settings route tree (grepped) — this is purely the BackCircle href-vs-history.back() mismatch, nothing else is interfering.

The codebase's only deliberate `pushState`/`popstate` history-management pattern lives in the wizard flows (`wallets/new/+page.svelte` L143-244, `wallets/multisig/new/+page.svelte` L755-816) and explicitly avoids this exact "pushState<->popstate loop" class of bug — Settings needs the same care.

## Fix

Change `BackCircle`'s href branch to use `goto(href, { replaceState: true })` instead of rendering a plain `<a href>`, OR have Settings sub-pages call `history.back()` when the referrer was `/settings`, falling back to a replaceState goto otherwise. Apply to all four sub-pages (tokens, devices, contacts, notifications) plus their desktop breadcrumbs.
close_reason: BackCircle href branch now uses goto(href, {replaceState: true}) instead of <a>. Breaks the settings back-button loop. All 10 callers verified safe.

title: addressWatcher.ts enumerateAll() does N+1 multisig_keys queries instead of a single JOIN
description: src/lib/server/addressWatcher.ts's enumerateAll() (~153-190) issues one SELECT * FROM multisigs followed by a per-row SELECT * FROM multisig_keys WHERE multisig_id = ? inside a loop — a classic N+1 pattern, run on every refresh cycle (startup + every 5 minutes). Not on the request path and SQLite queries are cheap, so today's impact is modest, but it scales linearly and needlessly with wallet count. src/lib/server/keyHealth.ts's scan already does the equivalent correctly with a single JOIN (multisig_keys k JOIN multisigs m) — mirror that pattern here.
close_reason: Resolved together with cairn-ggvt: enumerateAll() no longer runs a per-multisig SELECT multisig_keys inside the loop. It now issues one 'SELECT * FROM multisig_keys ORDER BY multisig_id, position' upfront and groups rows into a Map<multisigId, keys[]> in memory — O(1) queries instead of N+1. Equivalent to the keyHealth.ts single-scan pattern; a JOIN would also work but the batch-group keeps one row per multisig cleanly. Typecheck clean.

title: POST /api/wallets serializes badly under concurrent load
description:
Mixed-scenario load test (steady-browsing + rapid-fire + writes at 40/20/40 mix) shows p50 latency degrading 213ms -> 4,484ms as concurrency goes from tier 10 to tier 200. Throughput stays stuck around 41-59 rps across all tiers (does not scale with concurrency). It is the only scenario with any errors at all: 0.3% at tier 200. Server event-loop lag p99 hits 2,946ms at tier 200, tracking the latency curve closely.

Suspected cause: the sync xpub-parse + DatabaseSync insert path in createWallet (wallets.ts:288) serializing under concurrent POST /api/wallets calls, compounding the existing node:sqlite DatabaseSync event-loop-blocking issue (cairn-xlrm).

Evidence: scripts/load-test/results run-2026-07-12T23-21-48-110Z-*.json on branch test/qa-wave-2026-07-12, commit 8b7b110f384299bf11d1076de3dccadb8298ec82. Full results table and analysis in docs/TEST-REPORT-2026-07-12.md and docs/TEST-FINDINGS-2026-07-12.md on that branch.

Relates: cairn-xlrm, cairn-6xxa

title: Multisig PSBT construction + per-key signing stepper
description: M-of-N PSBT construction (witnessScript, per-key bip32Derivation on inputs+change), vault send flow with per-key sub-stepper: 'Key 1 of 2: sign with your Trezor' -> next signer, live progress, quorum-aware key skipping ('Recovery key — not needed'), each key routed to the matching device signer (Trezor/Ledger/ColdCard/QR/file) via the existing signerContract, signature counting toward quorum, finalize at M signatures, broadcast.

Browser-verified: full 2-key signing walk, Ledger register-first vs fast-path states, resume, quorum enforcement. 525 tests, 0 check errors.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Driver half of part 2 committed: signVaultPsbtWithTrezor/signVaultPsbtWithLedger + buildVaultPolicy/registerVaultPolicy + readVaultKeyFrom{Trezor,Ledger}. 103 hw tests green. Remaining for this bead: y8k pt1 (send flow + stepper, agent running) and the stepper wiring of these two drivers + registration persistence API.

comments.author: fable
comments.text: Part 1 committed (8b15ac1, e0f5a5e, fbccd9a): vaultPsbt construct/combine/progress/finalize with exact M-of-N fee sizing, vault_transactions + API with atomic broadcast claim and quorum refusal, per-key stepper UI live-verified in browser (2-key walk, resume, reorder, registration callouts). 516/516 tests. Remaining: wire signVaultPsbtWithTrezor/Ledger (9149a41) into the Sign step's disabled cards + Ledger registration persistence route. Hook point documented: feed draft.psbt, call handleSigned(base64); server plumbing needs zero changes.

title: Concurrent double-bump TOCTOU race condition
description: feeBump.ts:166 — the one-live-replacement guard is a non-atomic SELECT-then-INSERT (unlike atomic broadcast claim). Two racing bumpTransaction calls can both insert a replacement. Only sequential already_replaced case is tested. Fix: use atomic INSERT...WHERE NOT EXISTS or SQLite exclusive transaction, add concurrent double-bump test.
close_reason: Merged fc9453c: partial UNIQUE indexes on (wallet_id/multisig_id, replaces_txid) close the TOCTOU window; racing bump loses to already_replaced instead of double-inserting. Load-bearing test verified (fails if index removed).

title: Trezor driver silently swallows multisig-change/cosigner-xpub parse failures; no logging in src/lib/hw
description: src/lib/hw/trezor.ts:1105-1129 (multisig-change-output detection fallback) and :1242-1255 (selectMultisigKeyForDevice cosigner-xpub matching) both silently discard parse/verification exceptions: a genuine "multisig policy mismatch" and a benign "this output is an unrelated recipient" collapse into the same fallback behavior, and a malformed stored cosigner xpub surfaces only as a generic wrong_device error, misleading the user into thinking they plugged in the wrong hardware wallet. Systemic: there is no console.error/console.warn anywhere in src/lib/hw/*, so even best-effort cleanup swallows leave zero trace if they fail unexpectedly. Fix: add a client-side diagnostic (console.error or equivalent) at these swallow points, and consider distinguishing "parse failed" from "genuinely doesn't match" in the resulting error/fallback. Found in the 2026-07-05 tech-debt audit (docs/TECH-DEBT-AUDIT-2026-07-05.md).
close_reason: Fixed in 7ebd68c

title: Tab navigation feels slow/laggy — sections hang before switching
description: From Alex's Umbrel testing: switching between main sections (dashboard, explorer, wallets, activity, settings) has delays that make the app feel frozen. Likely SSR waterfall: layout+page server load functions doing heavy/serial work per navigation (Electrum round-trips blocking page loads, portfolio scans, etc.). Audit the load functions for the main routes, measure what blocks, and make non-critical data lazy or streamed (SvelteKit streamed promises), cache-warm, or client-fetched. May improve on a local Core node but the default (public Electrum) experience should still feel snappy.
close_reason: Dashboard '/', /wallets and /explorer no longer block SSR on Electrum: their load functions now return the chain snapshot / wallet scans / blocks+mempool+tip as SvelteKit-2 streamed promises (top-level promises are not awaited), with skeleton UI (existing .skeleton class) painted instantly and stale-while-revalidate on invalidate (old data stays visible until the new promise resolves — no skeleton flash). Wallets loader also got a defensive catch resolving to a loadError shape shown via Banner. Activity/settings untouched (already local-only). Verified live: SSR HTML contains skeletons, hydrated pages fill in with real public-Electrum data on dashboard and explorer. Per-call Electrum timeouts left at 15s — streaming removes them from the navigation critical path.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Investigated 2026-07-06. The layout load is cheap (local SQLite), but three main routes BLOCK SSR on Electrum round-trips per navigation: (1) dashboard '/' awaits loadChainSnapshot() = getRecentBlocks(10) + mempool summary + fee estimates + hashrate before rendering anything (portfolio is already client-fetched, good); (2) /wallets awaits a full scanWallet/listMultisigSummaries pass for every wallet (warm cache helps, cold/stale cache = full gap-limit scan over Electrum); (3) /explorer awaits blocks+mempool+tip. Activity/settings are local-only and snappy. Suggested fix shape: return the chain snapshot and wallet scans as SvelteKit streamed promises with skeleton UI (or move them client-side like /api/portfolio), keep tip/blocks in the existing warm caches so navigation reads cache and refreshes in the background, and tighten Electrum per-call timeouts so one slow public server can't freeze a page load.

title: DB migration warning uses console.warn instead of the pino logger
description:
src/lib/server/db.ts (the rename-recovery migration hardening from 5a09851): when the empty-shell recovery detects the exact data-loss anomaly it exists to catch (a child table holds rows while its parent is empty) it calls console.warn(...) directly instead of using the pino logger built earlier this session specifically to replace ad-hoc console usage.

This runs at DB-initialization time, very early in app startup -- before hooks.server.ts's request-scoped loggers exist, but src/lib/server/logger.ts's base logger/childLogger can be imported and used anywhere, including at module-load time in db.ts (no circular dependency: logger.ts does not import db.ts). As written, this critical, rare, 'a human should look at this' warning goes to raw stdout only -- it will NOT appear in the rotated JSON log file or the admin log viewer, meaning an operator who relies on either of those (rather than watching raw process output at the exact moment of a server restart) could miss the exact anomaly this migration was built to surface. Swap the console.warn for logger.warn (or a childLogger('db') call) so it lands in the same structured, persisted log stream as everything else.
close_reason: done

comments.author: auditor
comments.text: Audit note: closed with reason 'done' (no detail). Backfilling: fixed in 5193aec — db.ts's empty-shell migration warning now goes through childLogger('db').warn with the offending tables as structured context, instead of console.warn. Verified.

title: GET / crashes with 500 when locals.user is null (unsafe non-null assertion)
description:
src/routes/(app)/+page.server.ts's load() does:
  db.prepare(...).get(locals.user!.id, locals.user!.id)
using a TypeScript non-null assertion on locals.user with no runtime check.

The sibling src/routes/(app)/+layout.server.ts guards the whole (app) route
group with `if (!locals.user) redirect(302, '/login...')`, but the page's own
load() never calls `await parent()`, so SvelteKit runs the layout and page
load functions concurrently for the same navigation. When locals.user is null
(anonymous request, or a session that just expired/was invalidated), the
page's load can execute and throw before the layout's redirect takes effect,
surfacing as a raw 500 instead of a clean redirect to /login.

Found via /admin/logs on the live v0.1.3 QA instance: a burst of 26 identical
ERROR entries within a ~17s window, all:
  { "err": { "type": "TypeError", "message": "Cannot read properties of null
    (reading 'id')", "stack": "...at load (.../(app)/_page.server.ts.js:39:81)..." },
    "errorId": "...", "status": 500, "method": "GET", "path": "/" }
— this exactly matches the `locals.user!.id` call site (two occurrences on the
same line). Not actively reproducing right now (the burst had already stopped
by the time this session started), but the code path is real and unguarded,
so it will recur under whatever race/expired-session condition triggered it
originally (multiple rapid GET / requests in a short window, consistent with
a polling client or health check racing a session invalidation).

Fix sketch: either `await parent()` in +page.server.ts's load (so the layout's
redirect always resolves first) or check `if (!locals.user) return { ...safe
default... }` / redirect defensively before the non-null assertions, so a
null user here can never 500 the dashboard.
close_reason: Fixed: src/routes/(app)/+page.server.ts's load() now awaits parent() before the locals.user!.id query, so SvelteKit resolves the (app) layout's load (including its redirect(302, '/login') when locals.user is null) before the page load can reach the non-null assertion. Previously the two loads ran concurrently on the same navigation, so a null/expired session could hit the assertion before the layout's redirect took effect. Not actively reproducing live (same as the bead's own note), verified via npm run check (0 errors) + full suite (1558 passed) after the change -- this is a race-condition fix, not a UI change, so code-path correctness plus regression coverage is the appropriate verification here rather than a live repro of a session-expiry race.

title: Admin > Users 'Wallets' count column omits multisig wallets
description:
Page: /admin/users

What I did:
- As admin, imported one single-sig wallet ("Native SegWit wallet") and created one multisig wallet ("Family Savings Vault"). Checked the Users table on Admin > Users.

What happened:
- My own row shows "WALLETS: 1", even though I have 2 wallets total (1 single-sig + 1 multisig).
- Confirmed via direct DB query: the `wallets` table has exactly 1 row for my user_id, while the multisig wallet lives in a separate `multisigs` table (not `wallets`). The Users-list wallet count is evidently only querying `wallets`, so any multisig wallets a user owns are silently excluded from their count.

What should have happened:
- The "Wallets" column should count both single-sig (`wallets`) and multisig (`multisigs`) records per user, since both are surfaced together as "wallets" everywhere else in the app (e.g. the unified /wallets list page, the portfolio dashboard).

Priority: P3 — purely a display/reporting undercount for admins, doesn't affect any wallet functionality or fund safety.
close_reason: done

comments.author: auditor
comments.text: Audit note: closed with reason 'done' (no detail). Backfilling: fixed in 7014a1f — admin.ts's per-user wallet count only summed the wallets table; now sums wallets + multisigs. One-line fix, verified correct.

title: Missing regression test: backup restoreBackup() silent-skip logging (cairn-sd3n)
description: cairn-sd3n (P2, closed in 7ebd68c) added log.warn calls (confirmed present at src/lib/server/backup.ts lines ~243/288/312/335) when restoreBackup() skips malformed/duplicate rows, instead of silently dropping them. No test in backup.test.ts asserts these log calls fire. Add a test: call restoreBackup() with malformed/duplicate rows per table, spy on the logger, and assert log.warn is called once per skipped row with the correct table/reason context while the summary count reflects only successful inserts.
close_reason: Regression tests committed in 730319f (portfolio.test.ts new, backup.test.ts extended with logger spy, chain/chain.test.ts new facade coverage).

title: Send flow: no pre-flight dust check for plain recipient amounts
description: DUST_SATS=546 (bitcoin/psbt.ts, mirrored in multisigPsbt.ts) is only enforced on the send-max sweep path and the RBF change-output path. Plain recipient amounts have no pre-flight dust check: constructPsbt with amount:100 builds and persists a draft silently, and only fails later at broadcast via mempool relay policy (confusing, late failure). Found (not fixed) during cairn-9v9g send-flow boundary matrix work; pinned in the 'KNOWN GAP' describe blocks in src/lib/server/bitcoin/sendBoundaryMatrix.test.ts and src/lib/server/sendBoundaryDraft.test.ts. MANUAL.md ~lines 810-829 documents the current (gap) behavior. Fix needs per-destination-script-type dust thresholds computed via outputVsize() (P2SH/P2WPKH/P2TR outputs have different dust floors), applied at draft-build time for plain sends, not just sweep/RBF.
close_reason: Fixed: dust pre-flight validation added to validateRecipientsAndFeeRate (src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts), shared by both constructPsbt (single-sig) and constructMultisigPsbt (multisig). 199/199 targeted tests pass. Commit 8bac15f.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
FIXED (server-side pre-flight only; not closing — leaving for Alex/QA sign-off).

Root cause confirmed: DUST_SATS=546 was a flat constant enforced ONLY on the
send-max sweep-result check and the RBF-replacement change-output check
(both in psbt.ts / multisigPsbt.ts). A plain (non-max) recipient amount had
no pre-flight dust check at all, so constructPsbt/constructMultisigPsbt (and
buildDraft/buildMultisigDraft on top of them) would happily build and PERSIST
a draft paying a sub-dust amount, failing only much later at broadcast via
mempool relay policy.

Fix (src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts):
- Added `dustThreshold(address)`, computed the same way Bitcoin Core's
  GetDustThreshold() does: exact output byte cost (existing outputVsize()
  helper: 9 + scriptPubKey.length) plus an assumed spend-input cost (67 for
  witness destinations - segwit v0/v1+, i.e. P2WPKH/P2WSH/P2TR - vs 148 for
  legacy P2PKH/P2SH), priced at the standard 3 sat/vB dust-relay rate
  (DUST_RELAY_FEE_RATE = 3, matching both Bitcoin Core's -dustrelayfee
  default and @scure/btc-signer's own internal default - see
  node_modules/@scure/btc-signer/utxo.js). Reproduces the well-known
  thresholds exactly: P2PKH 546, P2SH 540, P2WPKH 294, P2WSH 330, P2TR 330.
  Deliberately NOT reusing btc-signer's `dust` selector option — that prices
  a fixed generic input+output pair, not a specific destination's actual
  script (see the existing NOTE in multisigPsbt.ts about not tuning that
  option).
- Wired into `validateRecipientsAndFeeRate()`, the shared recipient/fee
  validation helper both constructPsbt (single-sig) and constructMultisigPsbt
  (multisig) already call as their single source of these rules (comment
  block at psbt.ts:229-234 predates this fix and confirms the sharing) — so
  one change covers both wallet types, verified by test rather than assumed.
  'max' recipients are exempt (their own post-selection sweep-result dust
  check already existed and still runs).
- User-facing message: "This amount is too small to send." (single
  recipient) / "The amount for <address> is too small to send." (multi-
  recipient) — plain language per Cairn UX philosophy, no "dust"/"vsize"/
  "sat/vB" jargon; tests assert this explicitly.

Tests added/updated (all passing):
- src/lib/server/bitcoin/sendBoundaryMatrix.test.ts: new "boundary: dust
  threshold (plain recipient amount, pre-flight)" describe block —
  exactly-at-threshold-allowed / one-sat-below-rejected for all 5 script
  types (P2PKH/P2SH/P2WPKH/P2WSH/P2TR) on single-sig, plus P2WPKH and P2TR
  cases on multisig, a multi-recipient "names the offending address" case,
  and a send-max-is-exempt case. The old "KNOWN GAP" describe block (pinning
  the bug) is now "formerly KNOWN GAP, now fixed" and asserts rejection
  instead of silent success.
- src/lib/server/sendBoundaryDraft.test.ts (integration layer — real
  buildDraft/buildMultisigDraft + DB): same KNOWN GAP block flipped to assert
  the rejection happens BEFORE persistence (listTransactions/
  listMultisigTransactions stay empty), plus a happy-path case at the exact
  294-sat P2WPKH floor.

Full run: npx vitest run src/lib/server/bitcoin/ src/lib/server/transactions.test.ts
src/lib/server/multisigTransactions.test.ts src/lib/server/feeBump.rbfBoundary.test.ts
"src/routes/api/wallets/[id]/psbt/server.test.ts" src/lib/server/walletsDraft.test.ts
src/lib/server/wallets.test.ts
→ 19 files passed, 1 skipped (regtest-gated E2E, unrelated), 587 passed | 1 skipped.

Territory: server-side only (src/lib/server/bitcoin/psbt.ts +
src/lib/server/bitcoin/sendBoundaryMatrix.test.ts +
src/lib/server/sendBoundaryDraft.test.ts). No .svelte/CSS/harness/Docker
files touched. Not closing this bead per instructions — leaving open for
review.

title: chainEvents.ts has zero test coverage
description: chainEvents.ts — 100% untested, chain.test.ts mocks it entirely. Controls invalidateTipCache() on new blocks (a regression freezes displayed tip/balances), connect/disconnect dedup, and outage alert debouncing with 60s grace timer. Fix: add chainEvents.test.ts driving a fake EventEmitter pool through connect/disconnect/header events, asserting invalidateTipCache called only on strictly-higher header, outage alert fires after grace timer, cancelled by reconnect.

title: Umbrel auto-connect Wave B: Bitcoin Core RPC auto-detect via Docker DNS
description: Wave A (cairn-hxfn, closed 2026-07-12, commits b16eea1/1e311c3) shipped Electrum-only probing via umbrelProbe.ts. Wave B — detecting the Umbrel bitcoind container (Docker-DNS hostname / well-known 10.21.21.x address, RPC port + credential acquisition per docs/UMBREL-AUTOCONNECT-DESIGN.md) — has zero beads. Seed-once core_rpc_* settings with chain_provisioned_by='umbrel-probe', preserving env-over-probe precedence from cairn-loq7. Unlocks Core-RPC-gated features (cairn-zoz8.13/.19 degraded-mode paths) with zero configuration on Umbrel. Evidence: no bead references Docker DNS, bitcoind detection, or RPC probing beyond Wave A's Electrum scope.

comments.author: alexl
comments.text:
DESIGN COMPLETE -> docs/UMBREL-AUTOCONNECT-WAVE-B-DESIGN.md (single-sig-full-wallet, uncommitted).

KEY DECISION: silent full auto-connect (the Wave A model) is IMPOSSIBLE for Core RPC. Evidence (getumbrel/umbrel-apps bitcoin app + getumbrel/umbrel-bitcoin, verified 2026-07-12): bitcoind sits at 10.21.21.8:8332 on umbrel_main_network, rpcallowip=<subnet>/16 so any bridge app can CONNECT, but auth is a hard wall -- the RPC password is cryptographically generated per-install and lives ONLY in the bitcoin app's private .env (injected solely into apps declaring a hard dependencies:[bitcoin] manifest entry, i.e. Wave A2/chainEnvSeed). No cookie file is exported to non-dependent apps, and bitcoind runs WITHOUT -rest, so there is no unauthenticated endpoint. A network probe fundamentally cannot obtain creds without the manifest dependency we deliberately do not declare (cairn-2ldr "no node required").

CHOSEN DESIGN: detect-and-surface, not silent-connect.
- New src/lib/server/umbrelCoreProbe.ts: credential-free JSON-RPC POST to 10.21.21.8:8332; a 401 (or 503/-28 warmup) uniquely fingerprints bitcoind. Seeds ONE advisory marker core_rpc_detected=umbrel; writes NO core_rpc_* connection settings and leaves getChainConfig() byte-identical (so Core stays cleanly "not configured" -- no silent 401 storm, never worse than today's CoreRpcRequiredNotice empty-state).
- Assisted-connect UI (extends cairn-mz9p card, NOT a new card): pre-fills url=http://10.21.21.8:8332 + user=umbrel from HARDCODED constants (never from the responder, so a rogue bridge container cannot inject an endpoint), leaving the admin one field -- paste the RPC password (copyable from Umbrel's Bitcoin app Connect screen). Submit runs existing testCoreRpc() before going live.

GATING (differs from Wave A): Core RPC is independent of connection_mode -- getChainConfig returns coreRpc* in BOTH public and custom modes. So gate on CAIRN_PLATFORM===umbrel + coreRpcConfigured()===false + core_rpc_detected unset. Do NOT gate on or touch connection_mode.

PROVENANCE: new core_rpc_provisioned_by (null | umbrel-env | umbrel-detect), kept SEPARATE from Wave A chain_provisioned_by (Electrum-scoped). Plus pre-connect marker core_rpc_detected.

SECURITY: no credential adoption off the wire; blast radius of even a rogue Core is read-only (Cairn only does getblock/getrawtransaction/gettxout/estimatesmartfee -- never sends PSBTs/keys/xpubs). Human password paste is the trust gate.

PAYOFF is complete: Umbrel enables txindex=1 by default (umbrel PR #305) and is unpruned by default, so a connected default Umbrel Core fully serves every Core-RPC-gated explorer feature.

SAVE-PATH BUG TO FIX (Unit B2): admin settings action writes core_rpc_* only inside if(connectionMode===custom) (+page.server.ts ~L101/L123-130), but Core is mode-independent; the assisted connect must persist core_rpc_* regardless of mode and must not flip connection_mode. The JSON endpoint api/admin/settings already writes them unconditionally -- align the form action.

PLAN: B1 detection probe+plumbing (inert, marker-only, independently shippable) / B2 assisted-connect save path / B3 provenance+assisted card (folds into cairn-mz9p) / B4 live umbrel-s15 verification gate (ties to cairn-x26e next package cut). Failure modes (IBD/pruned/auth-fail/uninstall) + full unit+live test plan in the doc. Non-goal: declaring the hard manifest dependency (cairn-2ldr).

title: Electrum client: add small connection pool (2-4 conns) instead of single shared socket
description:
Load test (docs/LOAD-TEST-RESULTS-2026-07-05.md, Headline + Recommendation 2) identified that ALL Electrum traffic for the entire app funnels through one shared, pipelined TCP connection, causing address/balance lookups to queue behind each other rather than parallelize (directly responsible for the ~17.5ms/wallet portfolio-scan cost compounding linearly with wallet count - see companion bead cairn-fd56).

Confirmed by code read:

Core implementation: src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts (428 lines) - hand-rolled Electrum JSON-RPC 2.0 client over raw TCP/TLS (node:net/node:tls), class ElectrumClient (line 74), one net.Socket/tls.TLSSocket per instance (line 81). No electrum-client npm package used; this is the only wire-protocol implementation. Public API: request(), batchRequest(), getBalance(), getHistory(), listUnspent(), broadcast(), getTransaction(), getMerkleProof(), getBlockHeader(), estimateFee(), headersSubscribe(), subscribeScripthash(), banner(), serverFeatures(), ping(), close().

Singleton wiring: src/lib/server/chain/index.ts - ChainService constructor (196-207) builds one ElectrumClient. Module-level singleton at line 601 (`let instance: ChainService | null = null`), lazy accessor getChain() (604-607, builds on first call, not at boot). reconfigureChain() (613-632) is the only place that replaces the singleton - nulls instance, closes old client; next getChain() rebuilds from fresh admin settings. Called from src/routes/api/admin/settings/+server.ts:66 and src/routes/(app)/admin/settings/+page.server.ts:80.

Call sites (~40 files via getChain(), mostly through the ChainService facade - transparent to a pool). Direct `.electrum` access that bypasses the facade (these need explicit attention):
- src/lib/server/bitcoin/walletScan.ts:49,52 - chain.electrum.batchRequest(...)
- src/lib/server/multisigScan.ts:165,168 (batchRequest), :333 (listUnspent)
- src/lib/server/transactions.ts:79 (listUnspent), :418 (broadcast)
- src/lib/server/multisigTransactions.ts:402 (broadcast)
- src/lib/server/stateless.ts:292 (broadcast)
- src/lib/server/addressWatcher.ts:315-316,360,607 (getMerkleProof/getBlockHeader/getHistory), :640-641 (.off('scripthash'/'header', ...) event listeners directly on the client instance)
- src/routes/api/events/+server.ts:20,90 - PINS getChain().electrum for the lifetime of an SSE stream and compares by reference (`getChain().electrum !== electrum`) to detect a reconfigureChain() swap and end the stream. Most identity-sensitive call site - a pool must preserve some notion of 'the client' for this comparison, or the check needs redesigning (e.g. a generation counter instead of object identity).

Config - already admin-configurable: src/lib/server/settings.ts DEFAULTS (4-15) has electrumHost/electrumPort/electrumTls/electrumTlsInsecure; getInstanceSettings() (38) overlays DB-stored settings table rows; getChainConfig() (78) returns effective values. Stored in SQLite via getSetting/setSetting (25-36). Admin UI: src/routes/(app)/admin/settings/+page.svelte + +page.server.ts, API src/routes/api/admin/settings/+server.ts (PUT validates + calls setSetting + reconfigureChain()). No env var path - 100% DB-backed. A pool-size setting (2-4 connections) belongs in this same settings table/UI.

Existing reconnect/retry logic (per-client, in client.ts): lazy connect-on-demand via ensureConnected() (113, dedupes concurrent connects via this.connecting promise); auto-reconnect with exponential backoff ONLY when there are active subscriptions (onDisconnect(), 197-223, backoff RECONNECT_MIN_MS=1000 to RECONNECT_MAX_MS=30000, doubling); resubscribe() (233) replays subscriptions after reconnect; per-request timeout DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS=15_000 (line 10) with no retry-on-timeout. No jitter, no circuit breaker, no max-retry cap.

Existing concurrency control: NONE. batchRequest() (322-325) does plain Promise.all with no cap - fully pipelined on the single socket. multisigScan.ts chunks lookups via BATCH_SIZE=20 (line 31) before calling batchRequest, but that's an app-level chunk size, not a concurrency limiter - still one Promise.all per chunk against the one TCP connection. No p-limit/semaphore/mutex/queue package anywhere in src/lib/server/electrum or callers (confirmed via grep).

Fix: replace the single `this.electrum = new ElectrumClient(...)` in ChainService's constructor (chain/index.ts:198) with a small pool (2-4 ElectrumClient instances, configurable via the existing settings table/admin UI) exposing the same method surface, dispatching via round-robin or least-busy (track in-flight request count per connection). Since ~35/40 call sites go through the ChainService facade, the pool can be transparent there. The ~5 direct-.electrum call sites need either (a) the pool class mimicking ElectrumClient's method signatures so `.batchRequest`/`.broadcast`/`.listUnspent`/etc. 'just work' picking a connection per call, or (b) updating those call sites to explicitly acquire a connection.

Complexity: medium-large. Core pool class is a moderate, well-scoped addition, but wiring the ~5 direct-access call sites and the events/+server.ts identity check correctly is where the real risk/effort is.

Risks/gotchas:
- Subscriptions (headersSubscribe, subscribeScripthash) are inherently stateful per-socket - decide whether subscriptions live on one designated 'primary' pooled connection or are replicated/deduped across the pool. addressWatcher.ts attaches header/scripthash listeners directly to the client's EventEmitter (lines 640-641), so this needs explicit design, not an incidental side effect of pooling.
- events/+server.ts's reference-equality check against getChain().electrum will break silently if a pool object doesn't preserve identity semantics across reconfigureChain() - needs a deliberate fix (e.g. generation counter), not just swapping in a pool and hoping the check still works.
- reconfigureChain() must tear down and rebuild the WHOLE pool (close every pooled connection), not just one.
- No existing concurrency limiter to reconcile with - a pool is close to pure upside here since currently all concurrent load funnels through one socket, but adding a pool without also adding admin-configurable pool size would just move the hardcoded-constant problem rather than solve it - the admin-configurability requirement (per this ticket's ask) is part of the actual fix, not a nice-to-have.
- Test coverage: check whether existing Electrum client tests mock a single instance in a way that assumes singleton behavior - pool changes could break test setup if so.
close_reason: Replaced the single shared Electrum socket with a small connection pool. New src/lib/server/electrum/pool.ts: ElectrumPool wraps N ElectrumClient instances behind an ElectrumClient-shaped, EventEmitter facade and is a drop-in for ChainService.electrum — so all ~40 call sites (facade + the ~5 direct .electrum sites: walletScan/multisigScan batchRequest, listUnspent, broadcast, getMerkleProof/getBlockHeader/getHistory, and the SSE endpoint's identity check + on/off) work transparently. Stateless requests (request/batchRequest/getBalance/getHistory/listUnspent/broadcast/getTx/getMerkleProof/getBlockHeader/estimateFee/serverFeatures/ping) round-robin across connections so concurrent lookups run on parallel sockets; ALL subscriptions (headersSubscribe/subscribeScripthash) + banner stay on a designated PRIMARY connection, whose connect/disconnect/header/scripthash events are re-emitted as the pool's own so wireChainEvents/addressWatcher/SSE listen in one place. Identity semantics preserved: ChainService.electrum is the stable pool object (changes only on reconfigureChain, which now closes the whole pool). Admin-configurable pool size (1–4, default 2): electrumPoolSize on InstanceSettings + settings.ts (electrum_pool_size), surfaced in getChainConfig (both modes), with a 'Connection performance' field on /admin/settings + validation in the form action and JSON API. Updated ElectrumClient type refs in chainEvents.ts/addressWatcher.ts to ElectrumPool. Tests: 5 new pool tests (round-robin distribution, size-1 = single socket, subscription-on-primary + event forwarding, reject-after-close, clamp-to-4). Existing tx/multisig/stateless tests mock ./chain wholesale so are unaffected. Full suite 1067 pass, typecheck clean.

title: PSBT infrastructure & transaction lifecycle
description: Foundational transaction-construction layer for Cairn's v2 signing feature. This is the first real departure from Cairn's pure watch-only positioning (README currently promises 'private keys never touch the server') -- that promise must stay true: this epic builds and moves PSBTs, never keys. Everything under HARDWARE WALLET SIGNING (separate epic) depends on this existing first, since there's nothing to sign without it. Order within this epic: construction before download/upload before broadcast; saved transactions and labels can proceed in parallel once construction exists.
close_reason: PSBT infrastructure & transaction lifecycle epic complete: construction (yph.1), download/upload (yph.2), broadcast (yph.3), saved transactions (yph.4), labels (yph.5), signing stepper (yph.6), plus the critical signer-substitution guard (cairn-973). Watch-only promise preserved — Cairn builds and moves PSBTs, never keys

title: PSBT construction (unsigned tx builder)
description:
Build an unsigned PSBT from a wallet: recipient address, amount, fee rate (reuse existing fee estimate plumbing from src/lib/server/chain/index.ts), coin selection over the wallet UTXOs (from the existing gap-limit wallet scan in src/lib/server/bitcoin/walletScan.ts), and change output derivation using the wallet own change chain (src/lib/server/db.ts already tracks receive_cursor per wallet; change derivation should follow the same xpub/scriptType pattern as src/lib/server/bitcoin/xpub.ts).

ARCHITECTURE CAVEAT -- read before copying Bastion patterns: Bastion (C:\dev\bastion\src\server\psbt.ts) builds PSBTs via Bitcoin Core RPC walletcreatefundedpsbt, which assumes a Core wallet backing every watch-only wallet. Cairn explicit pitch (README) is "works without your own node" -- Electrum + public Esplora by default, Core RPC optional and currently unused. PSBT construction here MUST work from Electrum UTXO data (blockchain.scripthash.listunspent) and manual coin selection/fee math, falling back to Core walletcreatefundedpsbt only as an optional accelerated path when a user has configured Core RPC (src/lib/server/settings.ts coreRpcUrl). Do not make Core RPC a hard requirement -- that would break the no-own-node promise that is Cairn biggest differentiator from Bastion and from Sparrow.

PSBT library: Bastion uses bitcoinjs-lib. Cairn already depends on the @scure ecosystem (@scure/bip32, @scure/base) for xpub parsing -- @scure/btc-signer is the natural fit to avoid adding a second, larger dependency family, but this is Fable call; note it as a deliberate decision either way rather than a default.

Security-critical: only ever handles public data (UTXOs, addresses, amounts) -- no private key material touches this code path at any point. Must avoid dust outputs, handle insufficient-funds and fee-too-high-for-value edge cases explicitly, and must not reuse an already-issued receive address as change.
close_reason: Implemented in 81b4127 (+ schema in dacbbf0): Electrum-native construction via @scure/btc-signer — no Core dependency, per the bead's architecture caveat. Coin selection with own-change-chain outputs, send-max, confirmed-only inputs, nonWitnessUtxo for legacy, bip32Derivation only when the key origin is genuinely known. Proven three ways: 10 unit tests incl. signing the PSBT with the published BIP84 vector key via its embedded derivation paths and finalizing/extracting; a live mainnet draft on the legacy test wallet (223-sat fee at a requested 1 sat/vB, 1,037 sats change conserved); and a regression test for a discovered fund-burning trap in the selector's dust option that swallowed sub-18k-sat change as fee

title: PSBT download/upload for external signing
description: Download an unsigned PSBT built by the construction step as a .psbt file (standard binary or base64, whichever Sparrow/Electrum/other PSBT-compatible wallets expect by default -- check what's most broadly compatible), and accept upload of a signed PSBT for the broadcast step. This is also the foundation the 'generic file-based signing' hardware-wallet path (separate epic) reuses directly -- build it generically enough that the fallback device flow is just this same download/upload with different framing copy, not a separate implementation.
close_reason: Implemented in 7504822: binary .psbt download (GET /api/wallets/[id]/tx/[txId]/file, Content-Disposition attachment — the format Sparrow/Electrum/ColdCard expect) and signed-PSBT upload (PATCH .../[txId] validates parseability, moves the row to awaiting_signature). Built generically so the file-based device fallback is the same endpoints with different framing. Verified live: 762-byte .psbt served, summary parses

title: PSBT broadcast
description: Accept a fully-signed PSBT (from upload, or directly from an in-browser hardware wallet signing flow once that exists), finalize it, and broadcast via the existing Electrum client (src/lib/server/electrum/client.ts already has the connection -- broadcast is blockchain.transaction.broadcast) or Bitcoin Core RPC if configured (src/lib/server/settings.ts already has coreRpcUrl/coreRpcUser/coreRpcPass stored but unused -- 'stored for upcoming features' per its own comment; this is that feature). Validate the PSBT is actually fully signed and finalizable before attempting broadcast, surface Electrum/Core rejection reasons in plain language, and guard against double-broadcast (e.g. disable the broadcast action once a txid comes back, and record it against the saved-transaction record).
close_reason: Implemented in 7504822: POST .../[txId]/broadcast finalizes via btc-signer, refuses not-fully-signed PSBTs (verified live: unsigned draft returns 400 incomplete 'Not enough partial sign'), guards double-broadcast by rejecting a row that already has a txid (409 already_sent), surfaces Electrum rejection reasons in plain language, and records the txid + completed status on success. Uses the existing Electrum blockchain.transaction.broadcast

title: Saved transactions (drafts, pending-signature, completed)
description: Persist transaction lifecycle state per wallet: draft (constructed, not yet exported for signing), awaiting-signature (exported, waiting on a signed PSBT to come back), and completed (broadcast, with the resulting txid bookmarked). Needs a new DB table alongside the existing wallets/invites/settings schema in src/lib/server/db.ts. This is what makes the multi-step signing flow resumable -- a user should be able to start a transaction, export it to sign on a ColdCard, close the browser, and come back later to upload the signed result against the same draft rather than starting over.
close_reason: Implemented in 40cf0d8: saved-transactions management tab on the wallet page (status badges, recipient/amount/fee, resume + download + two-click delete for drafts, txid link for completed) plus GET .../transactions/saved. Draft/awaiting/completed lifecycle persisted; completed rows kept. Live-verified

title: Transaction labels
description: Let users attach a free-text label to a transaction (sent or received) for their own bookkeeping -- 'rent', 'invoice #4021', etc. Store per-wallet, keyed by txid. Independent of the rest of the PSBT epic -- can be built and shipped without waiting on construction/broadcast, and is also useful immediately on existing read-only wallet transaction history (src/routes/(app)/wallets/[id]/+page.svelte) even before any signing capability ships.
close_reason: Implemented in 60394b9: tx_labels table (already in dacbbf0), ownership-scoped getLabels/setLabel, GET/PUT /api/wallets/[id]/labels, inline click-to-edit UI with optimistic updates on the wallet history tab, 8 new DB-backed tests (203 total green). Live-verified end to end incl. trim, clear, caps, 404/400/401 paths

title: Guided signing stepper framework (Create -> Review -> Sign -> Confirm -> Sent)
description:
Shared UI shell every signing flow (every hardware wallet device, and the generic file fallback) plugs into -- build this once, not per-device. Requirements from the guided-flow work:
- 5-step top-level stepper: Create -> Review -> Sign -> Confirm -> Sent
- The Sign step is itself a sub-stepper, one screen per required signature ("Key 1 of 2") for future multisig support even though Cairn v2 signing starts single-sig
- Per-device guided instructions live inside the Sign step and swap based on which signing method the user picked (copy examples: Trezor "Connect via USB, approve on screen." / Ledger "Open the Bitcoin app, connect via USB." / ColdCard "Insert the SD card, sign on device." / QR "Scan this QR with your signing device.")
- Plain language throughout -- "Review and sign", never "Sign PSBT"; technical details (raw PSBT, derivation paths, xpubs) live behind a disclosure toggle, not inline
- A confirmation gate before broadcast that cannot be skipped
